It's ambiguous. Both

 * `http://host/?q=://`

and

 * `http://host/?q=%3A%2F%2F`

are perfectly valid URLs, so furl has a choice of what to do here.

> But should not be decoded in query neither?

The equality

```python
url == furl(url).url
```

is not a goal of furl. There are many URLs that furl happily accepts where that
equality fails. Like

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> url = 'https://www.google.com/a b/?a=a b'
>>> f = furl(url)
>>> f.url
'https://www.google.com/a%20b/?a=a+b'
>>> url == f.url
False
```

Zooming out, is there a reason you'd prefer furl to encode `.:/` (and their ilk)
in query strings? Is `http://example.com/?redirect=http://www.example.com/`
rejected by software you're using?

This regression is fixed in the latest furl v0.4.3.

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl('http://site4dads.ru/')
>>> f.args[u'testö'] = u'testä'
>>> f.url
'http://site4dads.ru/?test%C3%B6=test%C3%A4'
```

Upgrade with

```
pip install furl --upgrade
```

I added Unicode tests similar to yours to make sure this regression doesn't occur again. Thank you for bringing this to my attention Kristian.

I see two possibilities for achieving this. First, create labelsets __per group__ or second create labelsets __globally__. Unfortunately, both variants have some drawbacks.

## Group based labelsets
Using the exisiting protocol groups to classify the labelsets looks like the best way. However, this complicates the labeling process for the end-user. There are three ways to present this:

![Group based UI](http://s32.postimg.org/p3vz8vuph/labelsets_per_group.png)

- Option 1 is a three level tree which looks way too overkill.
- Option 3 works with comboboxes. This is the most easy to implement but requires messing with two comboboxes to get to the label of interest.
- Option 2 is a hybrid of 1 and 3.


## Global labelsets
Having global labelsets will make the labeling process much easier. The GUI may look like this:

![Global UI](http://s32.postimg.org/82356sfud/labelsets_global.png)

Option 2 uses a single combobox and looks quite nice. It will also be, by far, the easiest to implement. However, having global labelsets leads to the question what the protocol groups are for. The logical connection between protocol group and label set gets lost. Nevertheless, since labelsets can be set per block the logical separation can also be achieved with global labelsets.

## Summary
Group based labelsets | Global labelsets
------------ | -------------
:+1:  Integrates with existing protocol groups | :+1:  Easy to understand for the end-user and easier to implement
:-1:  More complex implementation and useability | :-1: Raises question about the relevance of protocol groups

I think the main problem is, that labelsets bring a new logical level to protocol analysis that somehow collides with the existing protocol groups.

FP = FuzzyPSO()

You didn't say what size of image you are using.  That is the most important statistic.  Without knowing that the timing numbers are meaningless.  For instance, I just ran an image that is 751x1002 pixels and it takes 366ms on a 1080ti.  I don't know how big your image is but it must be a monster for it to take 8 seconds to process.

Moreover, when I run a batch of 20 of these 751x1002 images the entire batch is processed in 834ms, a considerable increase in images processed per second compared to the one image at a time speed of 366ms.

It is likely that the GPU hardware is already fully saturated by your super huge image.  The point of using larger batches is just to give the GPU more data at a time.  But if you pass in a billion pixel image then one image per batch is sufficient to saturate the hardware.  Although the numbers you posted aren't really self consistent so you probably have other issues going on.  In any case, you need to be more careful when you benchmark things and pay attention to details.

@pdknsk IMO that is too specific. `x_axis` and `y_axis` assume two things:

- The data in a `Curve` is two-dimensional
- The user considers their axes to be `x-` and `y-` (they could be swapped, or some other coordinate system, or just data, etc.)

But `Curve` can hold data in any dimension. `locate_x` and `locate_y` have the same issues.

----

It's not too bad to "write your own" `locate_x`:

```python
def locate_x(curve, value):
    if curve.dimension != 2:
        raise ValueError('Bad assumption')
    x_values = curve.nodes[:, [0]]
    new_curve = bezier.Curve.from_nodes(x_values)
    point1d = np.asfortranarray([[value]])
    return new_curve.locate(point1d)
```

Right, when you slice the `y`-axis (via `curve.nodes[:, [1]]`) they are no longer "very different curves", they are instead "the exact same polynomial", namely:

```
  0 (b_{0,3}) + 0 (b_{1,3}) + 1 (b_{2,3}) + 1 (b_{3,3})
= 3 s^2 (1 - s) + s^3
= s^2 [3 - 2 s]
```

Hey @penilessquant,

If I understand your strategy correctly, you want to select the top 2 securities based on price momentum and set the weight of the best performing security to 70% and the weight of the second best to 30%. If that is indeed the case, then bt currently does not have an algo to set weights as such.

However, you can easily add your own algo. Here is a quick one just to give you an idea:

```python
class OrderedWeights(bt.Algo):
    def __init__(self, weights):
        self.target_weights = weights
    
    def __call__(self, target):
        target.temp['weights'] = dict(zip(target.temp['selected'], self.target_weights))
        return True
```
So now you can use this algo to test your strategy like this:

```python
data = bt.get('aapl,msft,c,gs,ge')

s = bt.Strategy('s', [bt.algos.RunMonthly(),
                      bt.algos.SelectAll(),
                      bt.algos.SelectMomentum(n=2),
                      OrderedWeights([0.7, 0.3]),
                      bt.algos.Rebalance()])

t = bt.Backtest(s, data)
res = bt.run(t)
```

Hope this helps. Let me know if you have any questions.

Another assumption that turned out to be false ... I assumed the _o1, _o2, ... parts of image URLs were assigned by the order in which they are returned by Tumblr's API. Instead these "offsets" are determined by upload order and the API returns images as the blog owner has arranged them, which can be completely arbitrary.

Here is a test-post if you want a simple example for that: https://mikf123.tumblr.com/post/172687798174/photo-order-test . Images where uploaded 1 to 4 and I re-arranged them afterwards. gallery-dl will use "o1" for image number 4, which also has a o4 in its URL, and so on.

The `offset` value from gallery-dl is just a simple counter that goes up by 1 for every item returned, which would work if images always stayed in their original order and it also functions as a generic solution for audio and video files which don't have an `oX` part.

https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/commit/4a26ae32df4cadee82959ae390a815dd7685c07e adds an option which, if enabled, sorts photos of a photoset by the `oX` parts in their URL. This should assign the correct `offset` value for each image (except if one is deleted ...) and have them in the right order. (The default value is `false` for backward compatibility's sake)

Another thing that might be useful is the "middle-part" of a Tumblr filename, which is now available for format strings as `hash` (https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/commit/6b72be8ee6d66259c28367dc6d963ac6c9988fec).
The post ID (and not something like this middle-part) is used in the default filename because of the order when viewing these files in a file manager or image viewer. Post IDs are nice increasing numbers while these `hash` values are basically random.

(... and if any of you have better names for `sort` or `hash`, please let me know. I'm terrible at naming things)

nbcl = NaiveBayesClassifier(simpletext)

testworthy = []

I think previously it is not allowed to put .ignore() before a .fit(),
because I though usually you want to ignore a data only when you fit the
model and see the plots of the results. But then someone complained to me
so I changed the code so that you can place .ignore() before ,fit()

The latter two cases are expect to give you the SAME result. What
.ignore(t) does is that it reverts the model to an uninitialized state (a
clean model, like a model just built) and then replaces the data at time t
with None (i.e., missing data). So any fitting step before myDLM.ignore(2)
is useless and has no impact on the results after .ignore() is called. So
what happened in the first situation

myDLM.fit()  // fit a model (no impact on the third line)
myDLM.ignore(2)  //  if fitted then undo fit. Replace data[2] with 'None'
myDLM.fit()  // fit model

is equivalent to the second situation.

I'm pretty sure it's the `break` command here that's causing it to exit:

```python
def _hijack_tty(self, pumps):
    with tty.Terminal(sys.stdin, raw=self.israw()):
        self.resize()
        while True:
            ready = io.select(pumps, timeout=60)
            try:
                if any([p.flush() is None for p in ready]):
                    break
            except SSLError as e:
                if 'The operation did not complete' not in e.strerror:
                    raise e
```

As I understand it, we've got a list of `io.Pump` objects here - each one is reading from a socket and writing to another (e.g. reading from a TCP socket representing the container's `stdout` and writing to `sys.stdout`).

Inside the `select` loop, we're flushing each of the pumps that's ready to go. The pump's `flush` method says that it returns `None` "if EOF has been reached" - reading the code for `flush`, that seems to mean that either `write()` on the pump's writer stream has returned `None`, or `EPIPE` has been raised by either the reader or the writer stream.

If I stick some debug in and run a container with `seq 2000` as in the example, I can see that, whenever the program exits prematurely:

- `ready` has two pumps in it: stdout and stderr
- the stdout pump hasn't returned `None` from `p.flush()` (i.e. it's still going)
- the stderr pump *has* returned `None` from `p.flush()` (i.e. it's reached EOF)
- because `write()` on the stderr stream (an `io.Stream` object) has returned `None`
- because `io.Stream.write()` has been passed `''` to write, a case it [explicitly detects and shortcuts](https://github.com/d11wtq/dockerpty/blob/e336a8f7f28a2f1f4048603be28929c9787864bb/dockerpty/io.py#L129-L130), returning `None`

I'm still trying to piece together what's supposed to be happening:

- Is it OK that we're trying to write `''` to `stderr`? Even if it isn't, it doesn't seem like that should bring everything to a halt.
- Is it OK that `io.Stream.write()` shortcuts and returns `None` if you pass it an empty chunk?
- Is it OK that `_hijack_tty` exits as soon as one pump is done? We [changed it once](https://github.com/d11wtq/dockerpty/commit/6ded9260d9c7f9d4761fbc2d70281b994d159126) to wait for all of them to be done, but this resulted in hanging, so we [reverted it](https://github.com/d11wtq/dockerpty/pull/23). It'd be good to reason this out once and for all.

In effect, you are requiring a way to retrieve the list of all active `*Handler` objects.

[This line](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/blob/199e4d6a9607ddc39df325b81cb164b5193bd8cb/telepot/__init__.py#L1390) in the file `__init__.py` indicates that there is a field `self._delegate_records` in `DelegatorBot` holding all the active delegates. To be precise, `self._delegate_records` is a *list* of tuples; each tuple's *third* element is a dictionary whose *keys* are delegate's ID and whose *values* are delegates. Unfortunately, those *delegates* in the dictionary are not `*Handler` objects you want to get at. Those *delegates* are actually threads (or tasks in async version) wrapping around `*Handler` objects. There is no way to retrieve `*Handler` objects from delegates.

For what you want to achieve, however, I think of another approach. The way telepot delivers messages and events is by broadcasting all messages and events to all `*Handler` objects, and lets each `*Handler` filter for what they want to hear. All `*Handler`s have a `listener` object which performs the filtering. Normally, the `listener` is configured to listen out for those messages and events aimed at its parent `*Handler` only. For example, [this line](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/blob/199e4d6a9607ddc39df325b81cb164b5193bd8cb/telepot/helper.py#L1099) configures it to listen for chat messages with a certain chat id; [this line](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/blob/199e4d6a9607ddc39df325b81cb164b5193bd8cb/telepot/helper.py#L772) configures it to listen for events with a certain event space and source id; [this line](https://github.com/nickoala/telepot/blob/199e4d6a9607ddc39df325b81cb164b5193bd8cb/telepot/helper.py#L1125) configures it to listen for chat messages with a certain flavor *and* a certain from id. You tell a `listener` what to capture by giving it a *message pattern* or a predicate function. [Here](http://telepot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#telepot.helper.Listener) is the reference.

To achieve a broadcast (i.e. sending an event to all delegates), you can configure the `listener`s to be less specific. For example, you can design your broadcast event to have the form:
```python
{'_broadcast': { ... }}
```
and configure your `listener` as:
```python
self.listener.capture([lambda msg: flavor(msg) == '_broadcast'])
```
Then, when you use `bot.scheduler` to schedule such an event, all delegates should receive it, if all goes well.

Mind you, I haven't actually done such a thing. But it should be feasible within telepot's design. Any unintended consequences? I can't think of any, but can't say for sure. Like I said, I haven't done such a thing :blush: Good luck.

The most direct solution for acme-tiny to deal with this issue would be to create an instance of `ssl.sslContext` with `verify_mode` set to `ssl.CERT_NONE`, then pass it into the `urlopen` calls. That would disable certificate checking and therefore allow expired certificates to work.

Though that's assuming Let's Encrypt will verify a domain when forwarded to a URL which uses an expired certificate.

The intermediate certificate is integral part of signing a CSR, without its intermediate a certificate is just invalid @diafygi! Returning the intermediate certificate has absolutely *nothing* to do with the setup of the software using the certificate (e.g. Nginx or Apache); this is another, completely independent step of setting up the software (i.e. the question "Do I need `cert.pem` and `chain.pem` or just `fullchain.pem`?" I totally agree that this question shouldn't be part of `acme-tiny`).

You **always** need the intermediate certificate for **any** software using a certificate, it is impossible to use the certificate without its intermediate. Without returning the intermediate certificate, `acme-tiny` simply doesn't support auto-renewal of certificates! You will always need to *manually* check the intermediate certificate *on any renewal*. This means, that `acme-tiny` supports at most semi-automatic renewal of certificates. This should at least be stated in the `README.md`, allowing users to decide whether to use `acme-tiny` or not. The current situation just results in broken setups because users aren't aware of this.

If you can't write to the temporary file, you stop and don't overwrite the certificate you already have.

To expand the scenario:

You write to the temporary file.  This might fail if the disk is full.  If so, you stop, and you still have a certificate in the renew case.  If it succeeds, you rename the temporary file to the destination file.  If the filesystem is living up to its end of the bargain (which it will most of the time), if that's going to fail, the old certificate is unmolested.  And if it succeeds, your new certificate is in place.

In the master branch a new option was added: `filter_filterLabel`; I'm not sure when I'll make the next release available, but you shouldn't have to wait too long.

### Notes:

* The `data-` prefix *must not* be added in the placeholders.
* An existing & empty data-attribute (e.g. `data-label-extra`) will not add any content to the resulting label.
* If an included data-attribute does not exist on the header, it will be replaced by the header text.

### Use it as follows:

#### Default example:

HTML

```html
<th data-label="First Name" class="filter-match">First</th>
```

Script

```js
// ...
widgetOptions: {
  filter_filterLabel : 'Filter "{{label}}" column by...'
}
// ...
```

Resulting label: `Filter "First Name" column by...`

#### Complex example:

HTML

```html
<th data-column-name="last name" data-ordinal-number="second" data-default-filter=", using a fuzzy search" data-label-extra="">
  Last
</th>
```

Script

```js
// ...
widgetOptions: {
  filter_filterLabel : 'Filter the {{column-name}}, the {{ordinal-number}} column{{label-extra}}{{default-filter}}'
}
// ...
```

Resulting label: `Filter the last name, the second column, using a fuzzy search.`

So let's recap what we have gained here:
1. flexible subclassing of all algebra elements and functions for customization, including when using multiple algebra in the same process.
2. same for parsing and tokenization + structure error messages
3. some side nice to have: cnf/dnf and a pretty tree repr for expressions. 
4. some simplification of the code and no new statements anymore
5. TRUE and FALSE are algebra singletons, not globals.

What we lost:
1. you cannot instantiate expressions directly. You need to create an algebra first and used the configured types
2. no more support for anonymous symbols, 0 and 1 expressions: These can be created alright as regular symbols
3. no implicit simplify. It must be called explicitly

```
sudo rsync -av ../dist/lib/ /usr/local/lib/
```

Unfortunately I don't think so without some code changes

The ODBC connection string is passed as-is to the driver, so companies can support any keywords they want.  Unfortunately MS didn't do the easy, obvious thing and allow an "accessToken=xyz" keyword in the connection string, at least as far as I can tell.

[This](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/connect/odbc/windows/using-azure-active-directory-with-the-windows-odbc-driver) indicate you need to set it as a connection attribute before connecting.  Unfortunately it also shows you need to pass a binary structure (thanks MS!) which pyodbc does not support yet.

I could add this constant and recognize it as a pointer type, but it would probably be better to come up with Python data type that is known to be passed as a pointer (SQL_IS_POINTER).  In Python 3, `bytes` would be a natural choice, but I'm not sure what I'd use in Python 2.  `buffer`?  Perhaps ctypes?

```python
SQL_COPT_SS_ACCESS_TOKEN = 1256
token3 = b'...'
pyodbc.connect("Driver={..};Database=xyz", attrs_before={
   SQL_COPT_SS_ACCESS_TOKEN: token  
})
```

Encoding the token properly would require using the `struct` module, but I think an example would make it tolerable.

`python-pptx` doesn't modify the `app.xml` part, which hasn't caused a problem yet. So I expect all these changes could be skipped. The only thing I think it would affect is properties seen in a file browser, like the Finder on a Mac and Windows Explorer on a PC.

The `rIdN` values chosen have no significance, as long as they are unique and consistent. They are arbitrary keys. In a `python-pptx` context, you'd just choose the next available key for a new slide and be done with it. There's a method for that somewhere in the packaging API.

> 6. **/ppt/viewProps.xml**

`python-pptx` doesn't update `viewProps.xml` either. This one is safely skipped.

I ended up writing this code which takes a placeholder and replaces it with a centered image. It works for a portrait and landscape image. The image is scaled to fit the biggest dimension of the placeholder.

```python
def replace_with_image(img, shape, slide):                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
    if shape.width > shape.height:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
        #fill vertical and center horizontally                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
        pic = slide.shapes.add_picture(img, shape.left, shape.top, None, shape.height)                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
        newleft = shape.left + ((shape.width - pic.width) / 2)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
        pic.left = newleft                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
    else:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
        #fill horizontal and center vertically                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
        pic = slide.shapes.add_picture(img, shape.left, shape.top, shape.width, None)                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
        newtop = shape.top + ((shape.height - pic.height) / 2)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
        pic.top = newtop                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
    placeholder = shape.element                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    placeholder.getparent().remove(placeholder)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    return         
```

Since bacpypes is currently dealing only with IPv4 when presented with a 6-octet address, would it be safe to get the `Address` to represent `IPv4:port`? In other words, do you feel there is still a chance of ambiguity in its present form if we went ahead with the change mentioned in this issue? Please let me know if my proposed change could be made, or we should hold/reject.

----

On the larger topic of handling addresses for all families and breaking the parsing responsibilities, here's one approach to deal with it. Please feel free to consider it if you think it is suitable.

1. The baseclass `Address` can be built with any address input and would have a hex octet representation for itself `<Address 0x...>`.
1. The `Address` would further get subclassed into their specific families, with multiple hierarchies, like `MSTPMACAddress`, `EthernetAddress`, `IPAddress`, breaking further down into `IPv4Address`, `IPv6Address` etc.
1. The baseclass `Address` will have a _classmethod_ called `build` (for example), who would consume an input and dish out the address in its most specialized class. This would be done by handling the input parsing only at its own layer, and then engaging a subclass's `build` method on its way down.
1. To handle conflicts when dealing with addresses of same length, the build could also take a kwarg called `family`, which will explicitly override any logic and cast the address as per the supplied family.

Example:
```
>>> addr = Address.build(<18-octets>)
>>> print(addr)
<IPv6Address [<16-octets>]:<port>>

Internally, the flow would be Address.build()->IPAddress.build()->IPv6Address.build()

>>> addr = Address.build(<6-octet>)
>>> print(addr)
<Address 0x...>        # as there is more than one repr, casting has to be explicit

>>> addr = Address.build(<6-octet>, family=address.IPv4)     # address.IPv4 being some enum
>>> print(addr)
<IPv4Address <a.b.c.d>:<port>>

>>> addr = Address.build(<6-octet>, family=address.Ethernet)
>>> print(addr)
<EthernetAddress ab:cd:ef:01:02:03>
```

For packets coming in from the bottom layers, the `family` could be fed in based on the interface & address to which the stack is bound.

The functionality of `pack_ip_addr` and `unpack_ip_addr` would belong somewhere within the Address subclasses and need not be used in the address building process (may be for debugging). BVLL now has a way of directly consuming octet-string and need not call these functions. It'd instead just call `Address.build(<bvlpdu.get_bytes(..), family=<family_hint>)`

I don't have any particular reservations around how the ipv4 and ipv6 code would be structured. However, it'd be great if those submodules implemented derived classes with varying functionality (as much as possible), as opposed to those submodules being package clones (with copies of entire files). This is largely to avoid fixing common code issues in multiple subpackages.

It'd also be great if the application building process took care of binding the appropriate lower layers using may be the application's address as a hint (such that users would never have to explicitly know/import anything from bacpypes.ipv4.udp etc).

a few points:
1. GetFollowers and GetFriends should not have a `count` parameter the page (or batch) size. Instead they should take **kwargs, and pass those on to their *Paged brethren. 
2. GetFollowers and GetFriends should not take `cursor` since they return the entire friends or followers list.
3. None of the `count` parameters should have or imply a default value, since twitter may increase or decrease the count at its API level, which may leave the program not working (say, if twitter lowers the max value of `count` from 5,000 to 2,500) or suboptimal (if twitter raises the max value of count from `5000` to `10000`).
4. GetFollowers and GetFriends should have a parameter `limit` to indicate the max number to return
5. GetFollowers and GetFriends should take an optional parameter like `should_sleep` to indicate that the caller wants to sleep and retry. otherwise they return with however many they could get without sleeping (which may be 0).
6. note that there's a subtlety in the sleep logic: if I call GetFriends with an exhausted rate limit, then it will raise a rate limit exception, but if I call it with rate-limit of 1, it'll make one call and then sleep. I think the uniform behavior would be for GetFriends and GetFollowers to sleep & retry if called with an exhausted rate limit

I had a need for this functionality, and implemented the following based on the patch from @Zintinio and the current python-twitter code. I did not observe any differences between the data returned with this and the data returned by GetStatus.

However, I did encounter a bug in python-twitter's POST support: the setting of tweet_mode is not honoured. Adding `data['tweet_mode'] = self.tweet_mode` before the relevant `requests.post` call in `Api._RequestUrl`fixed this for me. Perhaps someone more knowledgeable than me could advise if this needs to be done for all POST requests.

```
def StatusesLookup(self,
                   status_ids,
                   trim_user=False,
                   include_entities=True,
                   map_all=True):
    """Fetch information for the specified status IDs.

    The twitter.Api instance must be authenticated.  You are limited to
    100 ids at a time.

    Args:
      status_ids:
        An iterable of numeric ID(s) of the status(es) you are trying to
        retrieve.
      trim_user:
        When set to True, each tweet returned in a timeline will include
        a user object including only the status authors numerical ID.
        Omit this parameter to receive the complete user object. [Optional]
      include_entities:
        If False, the entities node will be disincluded.
        This node offers a variety of metadata about the tweet in a
        discreet structure, including: user_mentions, urls, and
        hashtags. [Optional]
      map_all:
        Tweets that do not exist or cannot be viewed by the current user
        will have their key represented but with an explicitly null value
        paired with it. [Optional]

    Returns:
      A list of twitter.Status instances representing the status messages
    """

    url = '%s/statuses/lookup.json' % (self.base_url)

    idstring = ','.join(str(enf_type('status_id', int, status_id))
                        for status_id in status_ids)
    parameters = {
        'id': idstring,
        'trim_user': enf_type('trim_user', bool, trim_user),
        'include_entities': enf_type('include_entities', bool,
                                     include_entities),
        'map': enf_type('map_all', bool, map_all)
    }

    resp = self._RequestUrl(url, 'POST', data=parameters)
    data = self._ParseAndCheckTwitter(resp.content.decode('utf-8'))

    return [(twitter.Status.NewFromJsonDict(data['id'][status_id])
             if data['id'][status_id]
             else None)
            for status_id in (str(s) for s in status_ids)]
```

Your second question: 

> Also is it possible to execute a close order on the day the order was submitted? The idea is that i want to make an order right before the closing and to capture the overnight price change.

The answer is *No* if you are using daily data and and execute the orders on a daily basis. You can only evaluate a "daily" bar when the day is closed and by then you cannot issue a "Close" order, because the day is over. That's why the `broker` in `backtrader` executes the order the next day.

But the answer is "Yes" if you are using "intraday" data and issue a "Close" order for example at 13:00 (for whatever reason) and the market is closing at 17:30. The order would get executed at 17:30 (your system could still cancel the order at anytime during 13:00 and 17:30)

The change to `writer.py` in 0.7 was the result of adding support for "quote" slides, which only show a quote with attribution (see https://github.com/nyergler/hieroglyph/pull/76). These are roughly the equivalent of a `blockquote` in HTML. RST uses indentation to denote quoting (http://docutils.sourceforge.net/docs/ref/rst/restructuredtext.html#block-quotes). With your example, you're actually getting a `blockquote` element emitted when you generate HTML, but browsers ignore it since it contains a subsequent `div`.

I'm uncertain what the best approach here is; just remove the exception that's raised, let it look goofy on the slide? Emit a warning instead of an exception, alerting the user that they have a quote, which may look strange?

I totally agree with the idea, but I don't like that it breaks the recursive nature of the lookups.

In particular, [this test](https://github.com/plumdog/flask_table/blob/master/tests/__init__.py#L443) should still pass, where it is not getting a url kwarg from the item directly, but from a related item using `_recursive_getattr`. By doing the `hasattr` check, this will break.

I would instead suggest that we add an optional `url_kwargs_extras` argument for `LinkCol` that is a dict. This should have keys that are the parameters for the url and values that should be added. We would then merge these dicts before passing them to `url_for`.

By keeping these two dicts separate, we don't have to do any guessing/assuming about what the strings actually mean. And we're being explicit rather than implicit, so we score more Python-Zen points too.

I have gone through all the freetype-py shipped examples, as well as the rather poor example above,  and modified them to be leak-free; however, I do not think the shipped examples should be unnecessarily complicated, nor should they try to be perfect. Consider that the python interpreter itself has a 15MB memory foot print, a 9k leak while it runs is quite acceptable and for the sake of simplicity. So:

- a bundle of problems I have fixed and improvements I have found while looking at all the examples are added to https://github.com/rougier/freetype-py/pull/52

- if you don't know the difference between `destroy=False` and `destroy=True`, you should just use the latter. `destroy=True` does not need extra code for memory management. `destroy=False` needs a bit more code before and after.

- I have modified the shipped examples to use `destroy=True` in https://github.com/rougier/freetype-py/pull/52 as they do not need the functionalities offered by  `destroy=False` , AFAIC.

- I am not pushing any of the detailed memory usage hourse-keep addition to the freetype-py examples out, as I think that would make the examples unnecessarily complicated.

- my first response is exactly the same as my final response - if you use  `destroy=False` , you should call `FT_Done_Glyph` explicitly yourself.  Your code is 26-line long, and my leak-free version is 47-line long, with 21 of the original lines (plus 2 with destroy=True changed). So I have doubled its length, and added the 4th case `destroy=False` of glyph.to_bitmap . You want your code to use 5 hours of CPU time without leaking? It is possible - you need to write a lot more house-keeping code - about double its current length. And let me repeat: if you don't know the difference between `destroy=False` and `destroy=True`, you should just use the latter. If you use `destroy=False` , you need more code, and call `FT_Done_Glyph` explicitly yourself.

A 26-line script which needs to be almost double in size, to 47-lines, to do its job is not a bug - it is incomplete and not yet finished.

At least one issue with this is that tags on github can be changed. By putting the release on PyPI avoids this issue. in addition (and somewhat relatedly), Fedora [prefers](https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/Packaging:SourceURL?rd=Packaging/SourceURL#Git_Hosting_Services) packaging from upstream provided tarballs, rather than autotgenerated ones from github. If the packages on PyPI don't contain tests or documentation then it is impossible to include them in distribution packages.

Okay, so we've set up a set of galaxies, with light profiles, mass profiles and redshifts. The mistake I've been making is trying to jump from the galaxies to an all encompassing 'RayTracingPlane' class.

What we need, are two intermediate classes:

1) ImagePlane - this is the set of coordinates that begins our ray tracing calculation (i.e. the image coordinates) and the galaxies that are in the image-plane (i.e. the main lens galaxy). By assigning galaxies to the image plane, we know how to compute deflection angles or make a light model in the image plane at its coordinates.

2) SourcePlane - this is the set of coordinates that we hit at each galaxy redshift (i.e. the image coordinates after deflection via foreground galaxies). Thus, for multiple galaxies with different redshifts, we will have multiple source-planes. A SourcePlane will be assigned Galaxys that define how a light model is fitted at its coordinates and how deflection angles are computed.

In the ray_tracing module, we can now have multiple classes for different ray_tracing setups (e.g. LensAndSource for just one image and source plane, LensAndSourceNoCosmology if we want to bypass cosmology, MultiplePlanes for multiple planes, etc.). If there are MultiplePlanes, the ImagePlane and SourcePlane can be set up automatically by the galaxy redshifts.The 'ray-tracing layer' therefore now takes care of all deflection angle computations. 

This layer has no knowledge of the image data (only its coordinates). The next layer up ('data_fitting' or something) will then take our ray tracing, image and source-planes, and use them to generate model image data and fit it.  

In https://github.com/Jammy2211/PyAutoLens/issues/11, I discussed how on a uniform coordinate grid we can make computation of things like light profiles efficiently, as the sub-gridding can be exploit the uniformity. This grid only occurs in the ImagePlane, thus we can directly tie these calculations to the plane a galaxy is assigned - its a lot easier.

Here is a slightly more realistic example of a Pareto chart:

```python
#######################################################################
#
# An example of creating of a Pareto chart with Python and XlsxWriter.
#
# Copyright 2013-2015, John McNamara, jmcnamara@cpan.org
#
import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('chart_pareto.xlsx')
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

# Formats used in the workbook.
bold = workbook.add_format({'bold': 1})
percent_format = workbook.add_format({'num_format': '0.0%'})

# Widen the columns for visibility.
worksheet.set_column('A:A', 15)
worksheet.set_column('B:C', 10)

# Add the worksheet data that the charts will refer to.
headings = ['Reason', 'Number', 'Percentage']

reasons = [
    'Traffic', 'Child care', 'Public Transport', 'Weather',
    'Overslept', 'Emergency',
]

numbers  = [60,   40,    20,  15,  10,    5]
percents = [0.44, 0.667, 0.8, 0.9, 0.967, 1]

worksheet.write_row('A1', headings, bold)
worksheet.write_column('A2', reasons)
worksheet.write_column('B2', numbers)
worksheet.write_column('C2', percents, percent_format)


# Create a new column chart. This will be the primary chart.
column_chart = workbook.add_chart({'type': 'column'})

# Add a series.
column_chart.add_series({
    'categories': '=Sheet1!A2:A7',
    'values':     '=Sheet1!B2:B7',
})

# Add a chart title.
column_chart.set_title({'name': 'Reasons for lateness'})

# Turn off the chart legend.
column_chart.set_legend({'position': 'none'})

# Set the title and scale of the Y axes. Note, the secondary axis is set from
# the primary chart.
column_chart.set_y_axis({
    'name': 'Respondents (number)',
    'min': 0,
    'max': 120
})
column_chart.set_y2_axis({'max': 1})

# Create a new line chart. This will be the secondary chart.
line_chart = workbook.add_chart({'type': 'line', 'embedded': 1})

# Add a series, on the secondary axis.
line_chart.add_series({
    'categories': '=Sheet1!A2:A7',
    'values': '=Sheet1!C2:C7',
    'marker': {'type': 'automatic'},
    'y2_axis': 1,
})

# Combine the charts.
column_chart.combine(line_chart)

# Insert the chart into the worksheet.
worksheet.insert_chart('F2', column_chart)

workbook.close()
```

I'm not very concerned, at this point, with being exactly the same as VCR. While I love the library and a lot of the opinions around it, I think this is just good UX to leave it as is.

> But isn't such behaviour of the default 'body' matcher a littble bit misleading? 

No it isn't. Most of the default checks are, by design, simplistic. They follow the rules of their equivalent checks in [VCR](/vcr/vcr). The body matcher by default simply checks equality, because that's all that is necessary if you do:

```py
import requests
import betamax

s = requests.Session()
recorder = betamax.Betamax(s, cassette_library_path='.')
with recorder.use_cassette('example_body_matcher'):
    s.post(url, data=b'my data to send to the server', headers={'Content-Type': 'text/plain'})
```

The person using betamax knows what they need far better than betamax can guess. In this very specific case, if I had built form-encoding parsing into the body matcher, I would only have done it for bodies that have `Content-Type`s of `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` and it would still be failing because your `Content-Type` apparently is `text/html` so it would be broken in this case anyway.

@sigmavirus24   The change to the core was necessary because the core code explicitly opened file handles in text mode (`SerializerProxy`), rather than leaving it up to the serializer class to specify the mode.  Since python's pickle writes a binary stream, you can't use the file opened in text mode.

Fixing the doctest was my initial idea too, but it doesn’t solve the issue. It especially doesn’t solve the 2 vs. 3 compatibility. It seems like calling `list(set())` returns somewhat more reliable sorting order in Python 2 than Python 3. On my machine the following code results in `['snap', 'pop']` consistently on 2 and in either that or `['pop', 'snap']` on 3:
```python
from __future__ import print_function
s = set(['pop', 'snap'])
print(list(s))
```
As an aside, funnily enough this variant of the code seems to behave more similarly on 2 and 3:
```python
from __future__ import print_function
print(list(set(['pop', 'snap'])))
```
But that just goes to show how sketchy relying on this behavior really is.

Back to the original issue: if you return a list and consumers expect a specific sorting, then we should give them better guarantees about this sorting rather than relying on the internals of the Python implementation. I wonder what PyPy’s behavior might be, for instance. My patch considers what the code actually does (treat the list of names as an unsorted set) and works on that rather than what it says it does (treat is a list.) 

As to the “safety” comment: [a search here on Github for getAxisNames](https://github.com/search?l=Python&q=getAxisNames&type=Code&utf8=✓) shows a handful of instances: no one is actually calling it, they are just copies of an older MutatorMath version. The MutatorMath docs never mention this method, either. 

An alternative solution would be to return an explicitly sorted list from `getAxisNames()`, I guess.

I can't believe the request name was wrong this whole time.

I removed the default mappings because they wouldn't work as expected since the PlaybackFailed request has a different format:

```{
  "type": "AudioPlayer.PlaybackFailed",
  "requestId": "string",
  "timestamp": "string",
  "token": "string",
  "locale": "string",
  "error": {
    "type": "string",
    "message": "string"
  },
  "currentPlaybackState": {
    "token": "string",
    "offsetInMilliseconds": 0,
    "playerActivity": "string"
  }
}
```
offset, current stream's token, and playerActivity are not top level parameters.

Also note that the top level ``token`` represents the stream that failed to play, not the current stream during time of error.

Some changes could be made to ``_map_params_to_view_args`` to allow mapping to these nested parameters, but for now can parsed with ``request['currentPlaybackState']`` as you said @appetterson

Each wormhole is separate from the rest.  Each wormhole has a nameplate.  Nameplates are randomly allocated from the range 1..1000000 (though with smaller nameplates preferred when possible).  

The nameplates identify a mailbox which can be used to exchange messages between clients.

Separately from the nameplate, each wormhole has a couple random words (currently taken from the PGP word list).  The two words provide 16 bits of entropy by themselves.  The words are not known to the rendezvous server.

To open a wormhole, a client encrypts some details into a mailbox for the nameplate they're using.  A client who wishes to have the other side of the wormhole must use the same mailbox and the same random words.  One guess is allowed per mailbox.  If the guess is wrong, that wormhole is ruined and the first client must begin again.

Thus, to reliably guess the nameplate of the next client (and thus gain access to the mailbox that client will use), the attacker must start 1000000-1 wormholes.  Having forced the next client to use a particular nameplate which the attacker can predict, the attacker must now also guess the magic words that client has selected (1 in 2^16 chance).  They have one guess and if it is wrong the wormhole is destroyed.

So, apart from resource exhaustion and denial of service issues, attackers aren't helped by flooding the rendezvous server with many spurious wormholes.

Actually it is usual behaviour. In Python 3 writing text in quotes is interpreted as unicode string while in Python 2.x it is a array of bytes. In 2.x if you want unicode string you have to write `u'...'`. That is another way to make it work in Python 2.x. pagrlare is fully unicode, meaning that it parses unicode string or `utf-8` encoded textual files. Actually, if you define your own recognizers parglare can parse anything. :)

So, the issue you were dealing with is more related to the change in treating unicode strings in Python 2.x vs 3.x. It is not specific to parglare.

Got some progress with custom `ifilter`-like functions. First attempt:
```python
def indexed_ifilter(wikicode, recursive=True, matches=None, flags=FLAGS,
                    forcetype=None):
    """Iterate over nodes and their corresponding indices and parents.

    The arguments are interpreted as for :meth:`ifilter`. For each tuple
    ``(parent, i, node)`` yielded by this method, ``parent`` is the direct
    parent wikicode of ``node`` and ``parent.index(node) == i``.
    """
    match = wikicode._build_matcher(matches, flags)
    if recursive:
        restrict = forcetype if recursive == wikicode.RECURSE_OTHERS else None
        def getter(node):
            for parent, ch in wikicode._get_children(node, restrict=restrict, contexts=True, parent=wikicode):
                i = parent.index(ch)
                yield (parent, i, ch)
        inodes = chain(*(getter(n) for n in wikicode.nodes))
    else:
        inodes = ((wikicode, i, node) for i, node in enumerate(wikicode.nodes))
    for parent, i, node in inodes:
        if (not forcetype or isinstance(node, forcetype)) and match(node):
            yield (parent, i, node)

def expand_2(wikicode):
    for parent, i, template in indexed_ifilter(wikicode, forcetype=mwparserfromhell.nodes.template.Template, recursive=wikicode.RECURSE_OTHERS):
        if template.has(1):
            replacement = template.get(1).value
            expand_2(replacement)
        else:
            replacement = ""
        assert parent.get(i) is template, (parent.nodes[i], i, template)
        parent.nodes.pop(i)
        parent.insert(i, replacement)
```

```python
def parented_ifilter(wikicode, recursive=True, matches=None, flags=FLAGS,
                     forcetype=None):
    """Iterate over nodes and their corresponding parents.

    The arguments are interpreted as for :meth:`ifilter`. For each tuple
    ``(parent, node)`` yielded by this method, ``parent`` is the direct
    parent wikicode of ``node``.
    """
    match = wikicode._build_matcher(matches, flags)
    if recursive:
        restrict = forcetype if recursive == wikicode.RECURSE_OTHERS else None
        def getter(node):
            for parent, ch in wikicode._get_children(node, restrict=restrict, contexts=True, parent=wikicode):
                yield (parent, ch)
        inodes = chain(*(getter(n) for n in wikicode.nodes))
    else:
        inodes = ((wikicode, node) for node in wikicode.nodes)
    for parent, node in inodes:
        if (not forcetype or isinstance(node, forcetype)) and match(node):
            yield (parent, node)

def expand_3(wikicode):
    for parent, template in parented_ifilter(wikicode, forcetype=mwparserfromhell.nodes.template.Template, recursive=wikicode.RECURSE_OTHERS):
        if template.has(1):
            replacement = template.get(1).value
            expand_3(replacement)
        else:
            replacement = ""
        parent.replace(template, replacement, recursive=False)
```

In your case, it's probably simpler to overide _autoregister().

def _autoregister(self, model, follow=None):
    follow =  follow or []
    if model === MyModel:
        follow.append("also_this_field")
    super()._autoregister(model, follow)

When restoring a revision, the change message is always "reverted to...".
This is because the django admin normally generates the change message, and
this change message generation isn't something that can be hooked into.
Short of re-implementing the entire admin change message functionality in
reversion, there's not a lot that can be done here.

@narasimha18sv Thank you for your report.
For this one, what you trying to achieve should be
```
out_path = plan_dir+os.sep+temp
terraform.plan(dir_or_plan=plan_dir, detailed_exitcode=IsFlagged, out=out_path)
```
or
```
options={"out": plan_dir+os.sep+temp}
terraform.plan(dir_or_plan=plan_dir, detailed_exitcode=IsFlagged, **options)
```
These will translate to output command
```
terraform plan -var-file=/var/folders/92/4fzkp03j4b3fff5rppp4ns780000gp/T/tmpr8e9mjba -no-color -input=false -out=<YOUR_OUTPUT_PATH> -detailed-exitcode
```

Because kwargs in python here is to accept an undefined keywords parameter, passing `kwargs=options` won't pass each key value pair in "options" as parameter, instead, it will pass a parameter called "kwargs" and its value is "options". See https://pythontips.com/2013/08/04/args-and-kwargs-in-python-explained/ for more references.

And in the library we intentionally treat "dict" type parameter as the variables we want to pass to terraform, because we assume that's the only complicated dictionary needed for those commands.
That's why you see 
`variables wrote to tempfile: {'-out': 'D:\temp\3603c548-57aa-4364-9d68-63a6582abd20\3603c548-57aa-4364-9d68-63a6582abd20'}` in the log

In version 0.4, I tried to stick as close as possible to flask-restful (mainly to ease the upgrades).
So prefix has been removed in flavor of standard blueprint usage (see exemple in #6 ).

In your case, you should write:
```python
blueprint = Blueprint('api', __name__, url_prefix='/1.0')
api = Api(blueprint)
app.register_blueprint(blueprint)
```
You can now register and call your endpoint like that:
```python
@api.route('/test', endpoint='endpoint')
class MyEndpoint(Resource):
    def get(self):
          return {}

url_for('api.endpoint')
>> http://localhost:5000/1.0/test
```

Two things to do here:

- [ ] If the `django_contenttype` table is missing we should return `None`. It should be safe as no relations could be pointing to the table yet anyway.
- [ ] If the latest `contenttypes` migration is not applied we should fallback to manual querying,

> prevents someone upgrading from the last version to the current version from needing to recode some of their applications

No, it doesn't. No one who uses this library calls `MailChimpClient()` directly. They call `MailChimp()`. The tiny fraction of users who *might* call `MailChimpClient()` are using the library in a non-standard way, and should expect to have their calling code break if they upgrade to a new version of the library.

> There is no functional reason why the signature needs to be changed

Yes there is. I tried doing it your way at first, but it radically complicates the backwards compatibility code, for no benefit. That's why I went with this strategy.

> Some users of this package may decide that the use of multiple username strings are better

**WHY??** The username *does not matter*. The old code did nothing meaningful with it, so the new code doesn't need it at all. If the developer wants to log a username on their own, fine. But `mailchimp3` has absolutely no use for it.

The code, as-is, contains many workarounds for multiple versions of Django. Some of these are to workaround deprecated features others to workaround internal API changes. I think the fewer of these workarounds required, the better for maintaining the codebase as the code becomes simpler and easier to comprehend. I've removed all workarounds I noticed in the PR.

Versions of Django before 1.8 are no longer supported upstream or maintained. This includes security issues. IMO, a Django library should not be endorsing a version of Django that may contain security issues.

Dropping old versions of Django makes testing much simpler. When I was working on other PRs, I regularly introduced bugs on old Python/Django combinations that I haven't used in years. Testing on some of these old combinations is cumbersome and difficult. Realizing my mistake often required a full, slow round trip of Travis CI..

As new versions of Django are release, a few of django-annoying features have become obsolete as they're now builtin. For example, the `smart_if` tag is now the default behavior of the `if` tag. As a result, I've removed it in the PR. Another example, the `@autorstrip()` decorator is now the default behavior of `CharField` starting with Django 1.9. I think it makes sense to encourage the use of upstream features once they land to reduce the maintenance, code size, and duplication of this library.

Those choosing to stick to old versions of Django can also stick to old versions of django-annoying through requirements.txt.

Ultimately, up to you on how you prefer to proceed. If you decide not to merge this PR, no problem, feel free to close.

Thanks.

> There is however no good method that I know of of testing a Q object against a Django model instance before it is present in the database. 

As far as I know that's not required. At least, my current mixin simply builds a query that filters objects with the same tuple of unique fields as the in-memory object, and then dumps the `WHERE` condition into the query. If a record exists (with a different PK than the in-memory object, if it has one), it's not unique. I don't see how `Q` objects should make that any more difficult.

So if people want to do this right now, here's a ListSerializer that you can use for it:

```
from rest_framework_bulk import BulkListSerializer


class BulkCreateListSerializer(BulkListSerializer):
    def create(self, validated_data):
        ModelClass = self.child.Meta.model
        return ModelClass.objects.bulk_create([
            ModelClass(**attrs) for attrs in validated_data
        ])
```

Just use this wherever you would normally use BulkListSerializer. Caveats: doesn't support M2M, or anything else that requires individual creation. Also, PK setting is only enabled for postgres on django >= 1.10.

Hi Fabio, thanks for your comments!

The disadvantage of your suggested approach is that in creating the dict returned from `super()` proxy, we have already serialized many of the fields (which will then get excluded from the representation).  I'm currently using this package in an API where a few of those fields are very expensive to serialize, and some api clients don't need the expensive fields.  If they are excluded on post-processing of `to_representation`, the damage has already been done so to speak.  

You could be right that we may be able to exclude the fields, at a later time though.  I was following [an example from the official docs](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/serializers/#example) where the fields were customized at `__init__` time by popping the dict.  I'll look into writing some tests with a custom field that blows up when serialized, and see how late we can delay the exclusion action - the unwritten promise which I didn't really cover in the unit tests and neglected to mention in the docs is that unneeded fields don't get serialized at all.  

You're correct that it's destructive on the serializer instance, but since the instance is bound to the request context, and the [fields are not modified if we're not in a GET request context](https://github.com/wimglenn/djangorestframework-queryfields/blob/08b1cea8e7d43d66ea8c6e2c185b717bb0dea450/drf_queryfields/mixins.py#L21-L22), I can't imagine a situation where we could re-use the same serializer instance for an input context?  Could you elaborate on that kind of use-case?

Hi Zhehao,

That is a very interesting idea. The reason on why I wrapped it around S2L because most keywords that I need to synchronize with AngularJS readiness came from S2L, for example "Click Link". So instead of I need to call 2 keywords, "Click Link" itself and "Wait Until Angular Ready" on every click link action, I could simply call one keyword "Click Link".

The other reason, I would like to contribute the code back to S2L project at some point in the future, once everything more solid, have unit tests, and test coverage for it.

Zhehao, please let me know if you have any scenario that would benefit more from add-on approach, maybe we could figure something out.

```
wget https://github.com/google/protobuf/releases/download/v3.5.1/protobuf-cpp-3.5.1.tar.gz && tar --no-same-owner -zxf protobuf-cpp-3.5.1.tar.gz
cd protobuf-3.5.1 && export CFLAGS=-fPIC && export CXXFLAGS=-fPIC && ./configure -prefix=/usr && sudo make -j16 && sudo make -j16 install
conda remove protobuf
conda remove libprotobuf
rm -rf ~/anaconda3/bin/proto* && rm -rf ~/anaconda3/lib/libproto*
sudo apt remove libprotobuf-dev
sudo apt remove libprotobuf-lite9v5
sudo apt remove libprotobuf9v5
sudo apt remove libprotoc9v5
sudo ldconfig
```

When finished, do `ldconfig -p` and verify no more occurrences of `libprotoc.so.9` and `libprotobuf.so.9` occur in the output. After some investigation, I find using GPU is not faster because the batch_size tested is rather small, i.e, 16, 64, 256, 1024 and NDArray + GPU is not as fast as numpy in these cases.
I've used this script to test the speed:
```python
import mxnet as mx
import mxnet.ndarray as nd
import numpy as np
import time

# Warm up the GPU
for _ in range(10):
    a = nd.ones((100, 100), ctx=mx.gpu())
    b = a * 2
    b.asnumpy()

N = 100

# Test the speed
for data_shape in [(16,), (64,), (256,), (1024,)]:
    dat_npy = np.random.uniform(0, 1, data_shape)
    dat_nd_gpu = nd.array(dat_npy, ctx=mx.gpu())
    dat_nd_cpu = nd.array(dat_npy, ctx=mx.cpu())
    nd.waitall()
    start = time.time()
    for _ in range(N):
        np_ret = np.sum(dat_npy)
    end = time.time()
    np_time = end - start
    start = time.time()
    for _ in range(N):
        nd_ret = nd.sum(dat_nd_gpu).asscalar()
    end = time.time()
    nd_gpu_time = end - start
    start = time.time()
    for _ in range(N):
        nd_ret = nd.sum(dat_nd_cpu).asscalar()
    end = time.time()
    nd_cpu_time = end - start
    print('sum, data_shape=%s, numpy time=%g, mxnet gpu time=%g, mxnet cpu time=%g' %(str(data_shape), np_time, nd_gpu_time, nd_cpu_time))
```

@macisamuele Thanks for merging the PR.

As for the tests above: The output of large_pets(200) is a list so your isinstance check with a dict type will always fail and fallback to the more expensive Mapping isinstance check. When Bravado reads a response object most of the arguments to is_dict_like are of type dict and therefore short-circuiting that check is able to increase performance significantly - in some of my tests with larger responses I see an approx 25% reduction in overall time.

`isinstance(d, (dict, Mapping))` is evaluated as `isinstance(d, dict) or isinstance(d, Mapping)` and therefore also will also lead to the same evaluations (and a very similar response time) as your example above.

```
In [11]: timeit.timeit(lambda: isinstance({}, dict), number=100000)
Out[11]: 0.021892070770263672

In [12]: timeit.timeit(lambda: isinstance({}, (dict, Mapping)), number=100000)                                                                                                                               
Out[12]: 0.022366046905517578

In [13]: timeit.timeit(lambda: isinstance({}, Mapping), number=100000)                                                                                                                                       
Out[13]: 0.13477492332458496

In [14]: timeit.timeit(lambda: is_dict_like({}), number=100000)
Out[14]: 0.0332639217376709
```

Ok so now it's clear that this is caused by the use of an undocumented internal feature that shouldn't ever have been used in the first place, even when it worked. So it's not a bug in Migrate as I thought it was... except that if Migrate *does* take action to prevent the errors, then it probably should also warn. Doing one but not the other leads to the inconsistent situation where you have no warning with Migrate but the code breaks without it.

>  I think it's possible to give a message for the $.event.props.concat bug. 

Well that would be awesome.

> What should that message be? 

I can't help much here since I haven't fully understood what exactly is going on under the hood. I personally would be happy with knowing who is doing what that it shouldn't do (at the highest possible level, e.g. reading `event.props` if that is the object that is not supposed to be exposed by the API), and knowing that this will (or could) break if I remove the Migrate plugin (as opposed to being just a warning about some obsolete but still working feature).

Are you sure this is specific to `concat` btw? It looks like the problem is that `props` is undefined. Actually I don't fully understand how Migrate fixes the issue; I've had a look at the source and I see it adds elements to that array *if* it exists, so I'm surprised it fixes an error caused by it being undefined. But obviously I have no idea.

> What action should the user take with that message?

They have two options: either switch back to jQuery 2.x, or keep using the Migrate plugin (as opposed to what you would normally do which is using Migrate only temporarily until you get rid of all the warnings, and then remove it).

@vangheem I've been thinking a bit about the shim. I think this only makes sense if other patterns use the same require.js paths configuration file that Mockup uses (in `mockup/js/config.js`), which I don't think is the case.

Otherwise, if they have their own path configs, then those path configs will in any case have to be updated, because the path of the shim will be different (it'll be in mockup and not mockup-core).
In that case, we might as well just update those patterns to properly use pat-base.

However, if those other patterns have mockup-core as a bower dependency and have their config paths configured to point **mockup-patterns-base** to the mockup-core base pattern, then they should continue to work properly without any modification or shim.

core dump sounds like it's a libzmq problem. Those are pretty hard to trigger with pyzmq bugs.

> Is there any offline way to install PyZmq without using internet or compilers?

Yes, it should be the same for pyzmq as any other Python package:

1. download the pyzmq wheel
2. move the wheel to the machine
3. `pip install /path/to/pyzmq.whl`

The best way I've found to do offline installation for Python packages is to make a `requirements.txt`, then do:

    pip wheel -r requirements.txt

This will download (and build if necessary) wheels for your packages and all of their dependencies in the current directory. Then you can relocate the wheels to your target machine, and install those wheels:

    pip install --no-index -f . -r requirements.txt

Out of order...

> What's the use case where & ends up in the text and the expected behavior is that it comes out as whitespace?

This is just a fringe case for the changelog. I don't know if anyone would rely on it.

Under previous versions: `&` is transcoded to `&`, which is then left as-is. The transcoding only applies to the 3 special characters in `cgi.escape`/`html.escape` (`<`, `>`, `&`), which go from numeric encoding to the text encoding.

> If you think this is a necessary property for bleach.clean, I'm game for reading a compelling argument, but note that similar functions in other places aren't idempotent (escape, quote, and so on).

I don't think it's necessary, just that it needs a clear warning sign that people will be sure to see.  

> I'm interested in seeing examples of wysiwig editors that use Bleach in the way you describe.

I don't know any wysiwig editors that are integrated with bleach.  my general point is that wysiwig editors tend to generate html entity defs, and that capability is broken under this PR.  We pull in some html entities via a wysiwg editor *and* consuming some JSON endpoints for data.

The rest of your comments all all somewhat related, and I need to jump deeper into the parser and functions to try some ideas out, but...

There are two concerns with the encoding of `&`:  html entities, and urls in content.

In terms of html entities:

I wonder if "safe" html entities need to be encoded as it means HTML entities being treated differently than tags, and like tags HTML entities are well-formed html.  The security concerns in the test suite are based on the dangers of escaping or omitting entities -- not having encoded ones.  By encoding & in this matter this manner, bleach is making every HTML fragment "unsafe" and basically generates HTML fragments to mimic the "plaintext" view.  How could someone properly allow an HTML entity under this system?  They're all effectively banned.

Encoding entities also has a fundamental shift in functionality -- it supposes that the input is plaintext instead of an html fragment, and the output is HTML that looks like the input.  

There are a few ways entities could be allowed.

potentially all entities could be left as-is (I'm not sure about the safety of this.  i believe it is safe, though it seems like the whitespace control ones may be unsafe in some javascript engines).  

python's stdlib tracks all the 'named' entities and their codepoints `six.moves.html_entities import name2codepoint`, and those should definitely be safe for rendering as-is (as the text or numeric input).  I played around with trying to wrap `bleach.clean` through a middleware like function to transcode these, but that needs to ignore the 'unsafe' entities [`('gt', 'lt', 'quot', 'amp')`  a single quote is `'` but does not have a "name"]

In terms of urls in content:

i understand that `bleach` is merely guaranteeing content for an html context, but "upgrading" `&` to `&` in a url  isn't making a string safer and (as stated above) breaks the utility of the library since `linkify` can not be applied to content that has been run through `bleach`.  

your design choice means developer must save the unsanitized content instead of bleached content, so link rendering can be controlled via `bleach(linkify(unsanitized))` .  saving the bleached content to later control link rendering via `linkify(bleached)` is **guaranteed** to break links because of the `&` to `&` conversion.

In theory the treeherder api is setup to only parse json content type, but there must be something wrong there. Anyway, you can expect request.data to be a dict if you set the request format to json in your test (see [the docs](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/testing/#using-the-format-argument) for reference)

Regarding the propositions for the registration of change handlers,

- I am not too keen on using decorators for this purpose.
    - If traitlets are to be used by scientists to interact with widgets / plotting libraries, decorators are not necessarily the type of things they would be very familiar with, if they are transitioning from languages like Matlab...
    -  using a decorator at declaration time of the function is actually giving the impression that handlers must be  "special functions" in some ways, or that there is some magic going on. IMO, `toggle_button.on_change(toggle, name='value')` is already very clear. It shows that the `toggle` function could be any function if it has the right signature. I am not convinced that using a decorator instead would be an improvement.

- regarding the name of the function used to register handlers, I think that `on_change` is short and practical.

- regarding the signature of the handlers, this is what bothers me the most in the current implementation.
    - first, we should have the HasTraits instance triggering the event given in the signature of the handler. (I have a Pr adding this to the current `on_trait_change`).
    In the context of widgets, we often have to create closures to capture that variable. It is probably one of the most annoying thing with traitlets when using them with widgets, as soon that there is more than a couple of widgets on the page.
    - once you have the HasTraits instance, you actually don't need anything else for most use cases but (optionally) the name of the changed attribute.

    ```Python
   class Foo(HasTraits):
        bar = Int() 

   def print_bar_value(foo):
        print('New value:', foo.bar)      # no need for the value of bar in the signature.

    foo = Foo()
    foo.on_change(print_bar_value, name='bar')
    ```

    The only thing that would be missing here is the `old` previous value.

We tested this new option on a setup with a 6GB Data.fs + 48GB blobstorage setup (about 160000 blobs).

The required disk space for incremental backups is enormously reduced, as was the goal of this feature.  
Ie. 48 GB blobstorage backup for each incremental -> 211 MB blobstorage backup. 

The downside is that restore time (greatly) increases due to a full directory listing included in each incremental, regardless of whether the directory contents changed. A restore operation must thus traverse the entire blob tree for each incremental (about 1h on our setup), on top of the time needed to unpack the full backup that is being restored as a basis for the subsequent incrementals (about 3hrs on our setup; this seems extreme but keep in mind this is a full unpack to an empty directory, as opposed to a regular restore using rsync which only needs to copy new files).

Essentially, this feature seems to trade off storage space reduction with restore time. 

This is something you should be aware of when considering using this option: test it out on a representative environment and watch the restore timings. The debug option comes in handy for that.

```python
class WorktreeTestCase(utils.RepoTestCase):

    def test_worktree(self):
        worktree_name = 'foo'
        worktree_dir = tempfile.mkdtemp()
        # Delete temp path so that it's not present when we attempt to add the
        # worktree later
        os.rmdir(worktree_dir)

        def _check_worktree(worktree):
            path = os.path.join(worktree_dir, '.git')
            git_path = os.path.join(self.repo.path, 'worktrees', worktree_name)

            # Confirm the name attribute matches the specified name
            self.assertEqual(worktree.name, worktree_name)
            # Confirm the path attribute points to the correct path
            self.assertEqual(worktree.path.rstrip(os.sep), path)
            # The "gitdir" in a worktree should be a file with a reference to
            # the actual gitdir. Let's make sure that the path exists and is a
            # file.
            self.assertTrue(os.path.isfile(path))
            # Confirm the git_path attribute points to the correct path
            self.assertEqual(worktree.git_path.rstrip(os.sep), git_path)
            # Confirm the worktree directory in the main checkout's gitdir
            # actually exists
            self.assertTrue(os.path.isdir(git_path))

        # We should have zero worktrees
        self.assertEqual(self.repo.list_worktrees(), [])
        # Add a worktree
        worktree = self.repo.add_worktree(worktree_name, worktree_dir)
        # Check that the worktree was added properly
        _check_worktree(worktree)
        # We should have one worktree now
        self.assertEqual(self.repo.list_worktrees(), [worktree_name])
        # Test that lookup_worktree() returns a properly-instantiated
        # pygit2._Worktree object
        _check_worktree(self.repo.lookup_worktree(worktree_name))
        # Remove the worktree dir
        shutil.rmtree(worktree_dir)
        # Prune the worktree. For some reason, libgit2 treats a worktree as
        # valid unless both the worktree directory and data dir under
        # $gitdir/worktrees are gone. This doesn't make much sense since the
        # normal usage involves removing the worktree directory and then
        # pruning. So, for now we have to force the prune. This may be
        # something to take up with libgit2.
        worktree.prune(True)
        self.assertEqual(self.repo.list_worktrees(), [])
        # Confirm that the repo's data dir has been removed
        self.assertFalse(os.path.isdir(worktree.git_path))
```

```python
wcapi = API(
```

```python
wcapi = API(
```

Not at all, you are doing it right. The explicit `--dev` flags is a design decision. The idea is that this prevents you from accidentally installing things you don’t want (e.g. in production) without noticing, while forgetting to supply `--dev` is more noticible because you’d see clear error messages when something is missing.

While the intention is good, I agree this is certainly not ergonomically optimal. A switch to flip the behaviour of _all_ projects on a machine sounds like the wrong solution to me, however. Maybe we should store this information somewhere in a per-environment basis, so if a project is initialised with `--dev`, subsequent `sync` would default to add `--dev` (suppressable with `--no-dev`). Or perhaps we can inspect the environment (like `pipenv clean` does), and put up a message like _hey this seems like a dev environment, maybe you want to supply `--dev`_?

Thanks for the suggestion! We will try to ponder on what the best design is, and figure out the implementation.

@tsiq-oliverc I have to say, your comments inspired me and I know they contributed to higher level of reproducibility of proposed solution.

Following is related to your proposal to put lockfile (`Pipfile.lock`) into repo to ensure repeatibility:

re **Hiding broken setup.py dependencies.**. The `pipenv install -e .` follow what I propose, but note, that this is not usage of `Pipfile.lock`, it is method to (re)create it. If someone keeps `Pipenv.lock` and use it to create virtualenv before installing the package, the problem is present.

re **Dependencies are likely to be invalid for different python versions or in another OS**. Examples are many: `doit` installed for Python 2.7 must be older version as newer one dropped support for Python 2.x. `watchdog` dependency requires platform dependent libraries: inotify on Linux, something else on Windows, something else on OSX. My former client used to say "This will never happen" and in 50% of situation it happened within 2 weeks. This is not the best practice for CI scripts.

re **Developers are forced to update ..** Imagine Open Source library with 15 contributors. It is so easy to forget regenerating `Pipfile.lock` by a newcomer or tired core developer. E.g. in `maya` package I was asked to regenerate the `Pipfile.lock` as new dependency was added to `setup.py`. Was that necessary? Did I update it properly? Did I update it for all execution contexts supported? Answers are no, not sure, no. Anyway, thanks for your proposal (it inspired me for solution described next to your comment).

re **Competing with tox**: Tox allows creation of multiple virtualenvs and automation of running tests within them. Typical `tox.ini` defines different virtualenvs for python 2.7, 3.4, 3.5, 3.6 and any other you need, and allows installing the package there and run the test suite. It is established powertool of serious testers. `pipenv` is not the tool for this purpose, but may interfere in installing things needed. In a way I followed your advice and proposed to use superior tool (tox) over `pipenv` where possible.

re **Pipenv fails.** This is really unfortunate. I had CI test (tox based) which run well on localhost, but when run via Travis, it failed due to `pipenv` issue. If I want to use it now, pinning does not help until fix is released. But this is how it goes - I will wait.

Just to add to this, in my issue (#2445) I proposed the best API for me would be:

Describe the solution you'd like
pipenv install -t path/to/src <package> installs a dependency into Pipfile/Pipfile.lock, and runs pip install -t path/to/src <package>.

I think it'd make sense not to do the equivalent of pipenv install <package> at the same time, ie it'd be like only running pip -t and match its behaviour. Just the dependency would end up managed by pipenv.

Note that pip does't have a pip uninstall -t, I think it'd be okay not to have a pipenv uninstall -t also (though pipenv uninstall <package> should still remove it from Pipfile).

--

This would make pipenv really nice for managing AWS lambdas, where you need to bundle your dependencies with the source to deploy, but you might want to do a normal `pipenv install` of the dependencies to run tests (outside of the deployed directory).
