I took some time and tried to assemble an ideal TOML pipfile example.

```toml
[sources]
pypi = "https://pypi.org"
legacy = {url="http://pypi.python.org/", verify_ssl=false}

# "main" is the default group.
[packages.main]
django = "~= 1.10"

[packages.py27]
django = {version="~= 1.9", source="legacy"}

[packages.py36]
django = "~= 1.11"

[packages.pypy]
django = "~= 1.8"

[packages.linux]
linux-spicific-pkg = "1.0"

# Group parameters.
[groups]
py27 = {version="~= 2.7"}
py36 = {version="~= 3.6"}
pypy = {python="pypy"}

# Pinned versions, updated automatically by pip.
[versions.main]
django = "1.10.0"

[versions.py27]
django = "1.10.0"

[versions.py36]
django = "1.11.1"

[versions.py36]
django = "1.9.7"
```

Here all version specifiers use [PEP-04440 version specifiers](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/#version-specifiers).

All packages should be defined in `[packages.<group>]` section, where `main` is the default group.

Group parameters can be defined in `[groups]` section.

In addition, I think it is a good idea, to save pinned version in the same single TOML file. This would work by identifying `[version.<group>]` sections and updating values in an automated way.  Automated pinned version updates should leave all other sections with comments untouched.

`[groups]` section parameters would have this options: *version* - python version specifier and *python* - python implementation (cpython, pypy, ...).

Tried to look to several example projects and wrote TOML files:

**Sanic**

https://github.com/channelcat/sanic

```toml
[packages.main]
uvloop = ">= 0.5.3"
httptools = ">= 0.0.9"
ujson = ">= 1.35"
aiofiles = ">= 0.3.0"
multidict = ">= 2.0"

[packages.dev]
httptools = ""
ujson = ""
uvloop = ""
aiohttp = ""
aiocache = ""
pytest = ""
coverage = ""
tox = ""
gunicorn = ""
bottle = ""
kyoukai = ""
falcon = ""
tornado = ""
aiofiles = ""
```

**Ansible**

https://github.com/ansible/ansible

```toml
[packages.main]
paramiko = ""
jinja2 = ""
PyYAML = ""
setuptools = ""
pycrypto = ">= 2.6"

[groups]
main = {version="~= 2.7"}
```

**Pandas**

https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas

```toml
[packages.main]
python-dateutil = ""
pytz = ">= 2011k"
numpy = ">= 1.7.0"


[packages.py3]

[groups]

```

**Fabric**

```toml
[packages.main]
```

Yes, it requires parsing again.

When using small fragments  (the parts to be extracted from a page)
the performance impact of parsing it again is negligible.

It's the endless gotchas when using html fragments that make it non-straightforward.

The resulting tree is unpredictable and whitespace is so inconsistent
that upgrading lxml breaks tests.

The hacks it takes to implement this are only a few
but are unintuitive and require lot of documenting examples and testing.

is something already solved by xpath, there's no need for a shortcut method.

One thing we might be missing here is incorporating the anomaly likelihood into the model itself (like the anomaly score).

If a user creates a model and passes data into it, there is a period of time where the anomaly likelihood is always `0.5` until it gathers enough data to start it's windowed calculation. If this model is serialized and restarted, this same learning period applies again. Ideally, this resurrected model would retain the anomaly likelihood data so it could continue where it left off instead of starting with a bunch of `0.5` values upon resurrection.

Hello. To answer your question - yes it is entirely possible to do that, however it would most probably involve creating new API endpoint, which would test if given token is correct or to update existing endpoint.

The problem is that in case if your user have not yet used the token he will call the endpoint twice (or two endpoints) and while such a simple check should not bring overwhelming load onto your infrastructure it still seems suboptimal.

Other solution which is more of a workaround might be to store used tokens at front-end, but here also are some pitfalls - e.g. user can remove cached data, limited to single device...

this is not a bug but a feature... (i agree that it's confusing, but it would break a lot of things in XML otherwise).

The reason for the behavior you see is that QNames and CURIEs restict the allowed amount of characters in the so called `LocalPart` a lot more than one would think. The reason is that they are intended to be valid XML attribute names. In other words: `<foo :first%20name="something" />` wouldn't work, cause the `%` is not allowed in that place.

Here's the spec: https://www.w3.org/TR/REC-xml-names/#ns-qualnames
Clicking through you'll arrive at https://www.w3.org/TR/REC-xml/#NT-Name ...

`hex(ord('%')) == '0x25'` is simply not in the char ranges allowed by NameChar.

Literals subclass unicode because they all have some string representation (_lexical space_). That does not stop us from having any python object as value (_value space_). For instance, xsd:integers have the string representation as a "2", but a as value a python int with the value 2. 

The same way, Literals can have a lexical encoding which is the base64 encoded byte-stream, and a value-object that is the decoded bytes.

I'm used to calling ```sigma=[det(T)]^(1/4)```, rather than having ```sigma``` be defined using the trace of ```T```.  Aside from the issue of terminology, I think it would be good to have the determinant radius be an option for this routine; if you have a galaxy that is a sheared version of a round one, the determinant radius is less sensitive to the shearing than the trace radius. Haproxy sends health-check probes to all patroni nodes it knows:

Look into config file: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/haproxy.cfg#L24 `check port 8008`

8008 is the patroni rest api port.
If node is running as a master, patroni will respond on request 'GET /' with http status code 200, in other case it will return 503.

haproxy sends traffic only to the nodes which responding with 200.

It is also possible to do read load balancing with haproxy, but for that it should do a different health-check: `GET /replica`

The out parameter to the `Radar.info` function is a file-like object (anything with a `write` method), probably the best example is a file opened for writing.  So you need to do the following:

``` Python
import pyart
radar = pyart.io.read('KTLX20130520_222418_V06')
file_obj = open('longradar.txt', 'w')
radar.info(out=file_obj)
file_obj.close()
```

This is a bit more work that providing a file name but provides additional functionality (printing info on multiple radars to a single file, logging, dumping to a gzip file, standard error, etc).   

We could also have the method accept a string filename which written to and add a bit of logic to do type checking.

I'm trying to provide back compatibility for this feature: https://github.com/pydata/pandas-gbq/pull/76. This is for preventing stalled jobs from hanging an entire script, and to have an elegant way to move on in the script without having to write our own timer function to handle; if the actual query can be killed in background, all the better, but at the bare minimum, it should stop looking for results after a set amount of time and move on. Unfortunately I can't replicate with aync_query, but I can with sync_query. 

As for seconds, I tried several more complex queries that definitely takes more than 1 second to complete and it still returned results for all of them. I'm ok with updating to seconds in my project since I can't imagine many use cases for sub-1 second timeouts being needed, but if it is indeed supposed to work this way, the documentation here should update to reflect this since there are no mentions of the timeout parameter being in seconds (and for legacy users and those perusing the docs, one might be mistaken that it's supposed to be milliseconds due to the timeoutMs and timeout_ms references).

@indexofire 简要地说，SS的原理就是先在服务器端开一个本地的Socks5代理，但是客户端不是直接连接这个服务器上的Socks5代理，为什么呢？因为防火墙很容易识别出这是一个Socks5代理链接从而给你随即丢包或者直接掐断。所以解决办法就是：让防火墙不容易发现这是一个Socks5代理。具体做法就是把客户端的流量通过一定算法加密，将密文发送到服务器，服务器解密密文得到原文，再把这个原文发到服务器本地的Socks5代理上，服务器返回信息也是一样的道理。这样防火墙就只能看到被加密算法加密过的密文，就不能有效识别这是个什么东西（也就是不清楚这到底是不是个代理链接），从而不会给你乱丢包（当然不是绝对没有）或者贸然掐断你的链接，这也就是SS相比于其他的代理要快很多的缘故。所以SS的加密**并不是为了保证原文的安全性，而是为了糊弄防火墙。**这就是SS的基本原理了，如果有不对的地方还请多多指教。

I've pondered this off-and-on.  Can you elaborate more on the sorts of things that would go into pexrc?  Some of them make sense e.g. PEX_ALWAYS_CACHE, PEX_VERBOSE, PEX_ROOT, PEX_PYTHON, PEX_PATH and so on.  Others that dictate the entry point i.e. PEX_SCRIPT and PEX_MODULE seem like they would only be problematic as the pexrc would taint subprocesses.  Perhaps explicitly disallow entry-point altering variables within pexrc?

The other thing that concerns me is the choice of precedence.  It almost seems backwards to me.  I think we'd want to prefer environment -> .pexrc -> ~/.pexrc.  If I've set PEX_ANYTHING in the environment, I'd for sure not want it to be overridden by any files on disk.  Also .pexrc in the cwd seems a bit too magical and prone to error.  I've got a bunch of tools in my $PATH that are built as pexes and I'm not sure I'd want their behavior to be altered if I happened to land in a repo or directory with a .pexrc.  Perhaps the .pexrc should be relative to the currently-invoked pex binary itself?

```py
im = np.array(im, copy=False, order='C')
```

~~~console

editor opens

save the file and quit

$ git rebase --continue # and repeat the amend for each commit
$ git push -f
~~~

So what's happening here is that fontmake always includes all masters in its MutatorMath designspace, and even if a master has parameters such that it won't affect interpolation at all, I guess MutatorMath still needs it to be interpolation-compatible with other masters or else weird stuff like this happens.

One fix would be to check if a master wouldn't be used for interpolation, and exclude it if that's the case. Another might be to fix something in MutatorMath itself.

@stephens999 @pcarbo implemented the discussion above:

1. Now strings in parameters will have to come with quotes, eg` tag: "eg1", "eg2"`
2. Unquoted string in parameters are default to "raw", that is, we can use `g: c(1/7,1/7)` in an R module, instead of `g: raw(c(1/7,1/7))`
3. The operator `raw()` is not removed and existing scripts with `raw()` would still work. This is not because of backward compatibility support, but because `raw()` is still useful, in situations where a string input as is will mess up the parser, eg: `value: raw(a1,a2,a3), a4, a5, a6`. 
4. Automatic data-type conversion is now restricted to only unquoted integer and float, to ensure basic query features still works on int and float. So `dsc-query` fields should remain intuitive.

Under the hood, we have now officially abandoned the pyYAML parser, after my unsuccessful attempt to hack pyYAML to do what we want. We implemented a new parser. It passes our limited unit tests, but I'm sure it is a lot less robust than pyYAML in handling corner cases. I did a big part of this over the past day or two. The general architecture so far, is to do it as a django management command that simply runs forever. As it runs, it iterates over all the PACER courts with RSS feeds. For each court, it checks if it has been run in the past five minutes. If so, it moves on. If not, it downloads the feed and checks the value in the `lastBuiltDate` field vs. the value that was found the last time that court was run.

If the value has changed, we process the entire feed, adding it all to the database and search engine, and then finally, marking the court as updated.

The biggest problem I see with this right now is that it is going to be absolute murder on our performance. For example, many of these feeds have hundreds or even thousands of items. The one with the most items right now has about 3,000 items. But even if we figure that they only have 500 on average, each, that's 500 items × 200 feeds = 100,000 items. That's...not good. 

I think the next step before I deploy this will be to add some form of early aborting code, sort of like how we have in our other scrapers. I think we just need to make some assumptions about what's new and what's old, and stop once we hit stuff we've seen before. If it means that we sometimes miss something that's entered into the middle of a feed, I think that's probably OK. Maybe we do a full check of the whole feed once/night or something just to be sure.

> I'm not sure you can get away with sorting keys and also claiming to not guarantee any particular sorting behavior

I agree with this, so maybe I should clarify my intent here.
- I think it's reasonable for us to guarantee things which we don't make part of the documented contract we have with our users.
- But those things are still guarantees we are making, and changing/breaking those guarantees is backwards-incompatible behavior

The only reason I want to not document it is that I think it will encourage foolish/dangerous behavior.
e.g.
```
# get filename + permissions, badly
$ globus ls $ep_plus_path -Fjson | grep -A1 '"name"' | cut -d':' -f2 | tr -d '",'
```

> Which sort of violates our soft-rule of passing through API responses as-is.

Isn't this rule already violated? We're deserializing and reserializing the data we get from the APIs.
If it came to us in sorted order, we might not respect that.

We could do this in SDK-land and have response objects load JSON data with `collections.OrderedDict`, but I'm not sure if that's right either?

---

> I'm fine with this though you really like sorting.

I'm not super-committed to it. I figured it was easier to kickstart discussion with a concrete PR.
We can reject this if we think it's a bad idea.

The reason I have for doing this is that it makes output more consistent, which makes it nicer for comparison and diffing.

So if you
```
globus ls $eppluspath -Fjson > somefile.json
globus ls $eppluspath2 -Fjson > somefile2.json
diff somefile.json somefile2.json
```
I think you'll get better results if we sort. Especially if those `globus ls` commands might run on different machines or under different CLI versions.

It's a weak rationale and we shouldn't pretend otherwise. I see this as low-cost.

# API

I'd propose to introduce `FromNetworkX` and `FromGraphTool` as sub-classes of `Graph`, such that a pygsp graph can be constructed from a networkx graph as `graph = pygsp.graphs.FromNetworkX(graph_nx)`. The `to_networkx` and `to_graphtool` methods of a `Graph` object would then return a graph object of the respective package. That is, `graph_gt = graph.to_graphtool()`.

For the second step, another sub-class, `FromFile`, will accept a path to a file and use networkx or graph-tool in the backend to load the graph. That is, `graph = pygsp.graphs.FromFile('path/to/graph.format')`. A graph will be saved as `graph.save('path/to/graph.format')`.

In addition to the graph, we should import/export one or many signals. Signals are sometimes part of a graph, and it's useful to save them together (e.g., for visualization). In networkx, signals can be represented as [node attributes](https://networkx.github.io/documentation/stable/reference/functions.html#attributes) (weights are edge attributes). In graph-tool, signals can be represented as [vertex property maps](https://graph-tool.skewed.de/static/doc/quickstart.html#sec-property-maps) (weights are edge property maps). In both implementation, signals can be named. I'd thus propose to introduce a `Graph.signals` attribute, which is initialized as an empty dictionary by default. Users can either populate the dictionary before exporting/saving, or retrieve the saved signals by name when importing/loading.

## Considered options

While it's clear that export and save should be implemented with methods, I was not sure if the import and load should be implemented in the constructor or as a factory. Options:
1. `graph = pygsp.graphs.Graph('/path/to/graph.format')`: constructor accepts multiple objects, such as paths, numpy array (adjacency matrix), networkx and graph-tool graphs. That is convenient but the documentation might not be clear as Python lacks function overloading.
2. `graph = pygsp.graphs.Graph.load('/path/to/graph.format')`: a [`@classmethod` factory](https://stackoverflow.com/a/12179752/3734066) is used to build and return the object as the `__init__` constructor would do. Each factory builds a `Graph` from a different object.
3. `graph = pygsp.graphs.load('/path/to/graph.format')`: the factory is a module-level function instead of a class method. While that is more convenient for the user, it lacks a clear meaning that `load` will return a `Graph` object.
4. `graph = pygsp.graphs.FromFile('/path/to/graph.format')`: sub-class the `Graph` base class to overload the constructor. That's more consistent as we do it this way for graph models already. It's also more OOP.

Another option was for the importing/loading functions to return a dictionary of signals instead of storing it as the `graph.signals` attribute. Exporting would have been done as `graph.to_networkx(myfeature1=mysignal1, myfeature2=mysignal2)`. If those signals are to be exported and saved, it's however clearer if they are part of the `Graph` object.

# Implementation

Networkx and graph-tool should be [lazily imported](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/4177735/best-practice-for-lazy-loading-python-modules) as we don't want them as dependencies. That is, import them in the function definition not the module.

Unit tests should be created to test the implementation. I propose to do a round-trip (to networkx, graph-tool, various graph formats) and verify that the graph and signals are preserved.

Again, any suggestion is welcome.

Conda 4.3 implements a `path_conflict` configuration parameter.  The parameter can be set to

* `clobber`
* `warn`
* `prevent`

The intention was for the default value to be `prevent` in conda 4.3, but our repositories actually have a lot of problems, and we would have created a *lot* of very visible pain for users.  In 4.3 (and probably 4.4) the default value is `clobber`, which preserves the previous behavior before this feature was implemented.  In the future, we'll hopefully be able to transition the default value to `warn` and eventually `prevent`.

@cjw296 I didn't intend to indicate "won't fix" when I closed the issue. Just didn't intend to track progress on the larger and more general problem with this ticket. But I understand the frustration.  We'll keep it open.

For background, here's the issue as it stands for virtualenv right now...

Let's say I have conda installed at `/conda` and so my python executable indicated by `which python` is at `/conda/bin/python`.  That means the shared library that python runs against at startup/load time is `/conda/lib/libpython2.7.dylib`.  Actually, it links against `../lib/libpython2.7.dylib`.  That is, the link path is relative.

Say I delete that file though... The OS X system loader has a library search path similar to the executable path `PATH`. By default on OS X, this library search path is `$HOME/lib:/usr/local/lib:/lib:/usr/lib` (see `man dyld`). So, when I delete `/conda/lib/libpython2.7.dylib`, then when `/conda/bin/python` runs, the loader will end up finding a `libpython2.7.dylib` at `/usr/lib/libpython2.7.dylib`.  The problem is, that library is compiled for the *system* python at `/usr/bin/python`, and thus we get that symbol error.

Now let's look at virtualenv.  Say I'm working on myproject located at `~/projects/myproject`, and in that directoryI create a virtualenv with `virtualenv ve --python=/conda/bin/python2.7`. Now after `source ~/projects/myproject/ve/bin/activate`, `which python` shows `~/projects/myproject/ve/bin/python`. What dynamic library is that python going to load against? Well, it would be `../lib/libpython2.7.dylib` or `~/projects/myproject/ve/lib/libpython2.7.dylib`. Except that file doesn't exist. (In fact, `~/projects/myproject/ve/lib/` will probably be completely empty.)  So the loader falls back to the library search path and finds `/usr/lib/libpython2.7.dylib`. :boom: Symbol error.

> `functions --erase` now prevents autoloaded functions from being reloaded in the current session.

> For reference, we've already flipped the order once in 4.4.x with #6602

Yes, and, of course, we shouldn't flip that back.
> Except I'd like to avoid two python subprocess calls here.

As I see it, #6820 is not something we can do quickly due to compatibility reasons, and hence there are only two options for now:
1. Use @njalerikson's approach and run Python code twice.
2. Get and source the list of deactivate scripts in shell code and after that run the Python part.

But since the latter requires heterogeneous shell code, it would be a step backward from the previous code unification -- let's avoid that!
To counteract the added running time of the doubled Python invocation, we can
1. Speed up `conda`'s initialization generally.
2. Make that proposed `conda shell.posix deactivatepost` lightweight.

As for the first point, I did some profiling and we can reduce that time by approx. 33 % by avoiding some costly imports and unnecessary precompilations. Those only require some minimal changes -- I'll submit a PR for that.
If it's really necessary to look into the second point, we could make that a `python -m conda.activate deactivate_post args` or `python -m conda.deactivate_post args`. That way we can avoid the general initialization and have in `args` the needed information encoded that we extracted in the first, costlier `conda shell.xxx deactivate` call. Hence the `deactivate_post` step could be very simple/dumb as it only processes already prepared information.

This might be a demonstration of how misguided this sort of code is, but that would be a digression :smile:

This is a known issue.  The latest HTSLib is not compatible with the pysam version we have.  The recipe has been updated, but there is an old package that is incompatible.  

You should downgrade HTSLib and get the pysam 0.10.0-py35_3 package until the new pysam (for the latest HTSLib 1.4) version is available.

So we generally release pysam versions alongside new releases of HTSLib+samtools, so I would imagine a release in a month or two.  However, we can definitely review any pull requests towards fixing py37 in the meantime.

Header files are not added to the project. They are included by other files. Just like with manual compilation a header file is never passed to the simulator directly but included by the preprocessor when compiling the file that includes it. 

If you really want to force VUnit to add it as a regular SystemVerilog file that is supposed to be compiled instead of included you can use the file_type argument to add_source_files. I suspect this is no twhat you want.

NOTE: You need to set the include_dir when adding your other files that includes this header.

To clarify,

1. Create an ACR registry
`az acr create --admin-enabled --name somename --sku Basic`
2. Push an image to the registry
3. Get the URL for the new registry:
```
export DOCKER_REGISTRY=$(az acr show --name somename \
					--query loginServer --output tsv)
```
4. Create a new webapp (linux)
```
az appservice plan create --name linuxappservice --is-linux --sku S1
az webapp create --name someapp --plan linuxappservice
```
4. Configure the webapp to use the container image from the new registry:
```
az webapp config container set --name someapp \
     --docker-custom-image-name \${DOCKER_REGISTRY}/image-name:latest \
     --docker-registry-server-url https://\${DOCKER_REGISTRY}
```
5. Open any needed port(s)
`az webapp config appsettings set --settings PORT=8080 --name someapp`
6. Restart the webapp
`az webapp restart --name someapp`

The app will never come up. Looking into the Kudu logs, the app doesn't start because the image can't be pulled; it needs authentication info to do the pull.

My point here is that it shouldn't be necessary to specify the username and password for the ACR registry when setting the webapp container if they are in the same subscription and I have RBAC to read the credentials.

I decided to investigate the resource using the Azure Resource Explorer and I figured out that the property I was trying to set was located in different resource type, one named `Microsoft.Web/sites/config`. So I created the app service normally and then I proceeded to update this resource to set `virtualApplications` property like so:

    az resource update --name "contoso-az-1/config/web" --resource-group "contoso-web" --resource-type "Microsoft.Web/sites/config" --set 'properties.virtualApplications=[{"virtualPath":"/","physicalPath":"site\wwwroot"},{"virtualPath":"/api","physicalPath":"site\api\wwwroot"}]'

> Is there a reason you are using the generic resource commands to accomplish this rather than the dedicated webapp/appservice commands?

Well, I initially choose it because I wanted to be able to create an app service from scratch in one command and to be able to set the `virtualApplications` property. But it doesn't seem to be possible with the available options on both commands - `az webapp` and `az appservice`. Please correct me if I'm wrong.

> Are they missing the functionality you are looking for?

I'm not sure, but there might be a feature I wish I could find in them. I'd say that it's the option to set the virtual applications and directories, it seems to me that it should be on `az webapp config set`, correct me if I'm wrong. Because `az webapp config show` when given the name and resource group of a `Microsoft.Web/sites` resource type returns a JSON object containing a property named `virtualApplications`, and its value is what I managed to change using `az resource update`.

I think you're confusing this with another project? Ours is a python package: http://pre-commit.com

Here's the commit which changed this behaviour: 479eb518734f329

Which actually was before 0.1.0 was released 😆 .

Looking at the commit, before it was introduced pre-commit would lazily install repositories right before running the hook.  This caused some tight coupling between the hook installation state (the `Store` as it's called now in the code) and the respository.  It also had some non-ideal output which would cause execution to look like this:

```
$ pre-commit run
Trim Trailing Whitespace...[INFO] Initializing environment for https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks.git.
[INFO] Installing environment for https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks.git.
[INFO] Once installed this environment will be reused.
[INFO] This may take a few minutes...
..............................................Passed
Fix End of Files.........................................................Passed
autopep8 wrapper.........................................................Passed
Check docstring is first.................................................Passed
Check JSON...............................................................Passed
Check Yaml...............................................................Passed
Debug Statements (Python)................................................Passed
Tests should end in _test.py.............................................Passed
Fix requirements.txt.....................................................Passed
Flake8...................................................................Passed
Validate Pre-Commit Manifest...[INFO] Initializing environment for https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit.git.
[INFO] Installing environment for https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit.git.
[INFO] Once installed this environment will be reused.
[INFO] This may take a few minutes...
..........................................Passed
Reorder python imports...[INFO] Installing environment for https://github.com/asottile/reorder_python_imports.git.
[INFO] Once installed this environment will be reused.
[INFO] This may take a few minutes...
................................................Passed
Add trailing commas......................................................Passed
```

the choice was made to decouple these as the general case involved running a majority of the hooks -- this allowed the code to be much simpler (install everything up front) and avoid confusion later on in the stack (is everything installed? do I need to check if I'm installed already?).

While I agree that the behaviour you're describing _is possible_ (compute which subset of the hooks the current run needs, only install those), it's certainly not simple and not the 99% usecase.  Especially when you consider that a repository may have multiple hooks, in different languages, and with different environment directories -- the story becomes *much* more complicated.

The caching option in travis-ci _hopefully_ satisfies the runtime slowness due to this implementation decision so I'm probably going to close this.

aha!  So apparently requests does not treat the response as a redirect if the "location" is not in the header.  https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/vendored/requests/sessions.py#L99  ends up calling https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/vendored/requests/models.py#L643 which means it won't fail if the "location" tag is missing, and instead will bump it up to botocore and botocore then issues a new request.  I'll probably add an issue to aiohttp to be able to support this scenario.

If you have a full MathJax installed in nbextensions. That would be:

```python
c.NotebookApp.mathjax_url = 'nbextensions/mathjax'
```

As for the reasoning for bundling: we used to use MathJax from a CDN because the size of a mathjax dependency was completely unacceptable. That was suboptimal because it meant for most users, math wouldn't work without a network connection (e.g. on a plane). Only by using a stripped copy, following the MathJax documentation for reducing the size of a MathJax install, was it feasible to treat it like the rest of our js dependencies.

It's the same phenomenon as when you assign a mutable container as a default value for a function parameter — it'll get evaluated only once, and the result is shared across invocations.

The right way to do this is to assign a callable to `default`, like `default=dict` without parentheses. It will then be invoked anew for every instance.

Good question! I think this just comes down to parameterization, by which I mean we can produce the same iterates using any of these versions by setting the (hyper)parameters differently.

For example, take the autograd version:

```python
velocity = momentum * velocity - (1.0 - momentum) * g
x = x + learning_rate * velocity
```

We could move the `learning_rate` scale into the velocity sequence, to yield

```python
velocity = (learning_rate * momentum) * velocity - (learning_rate * (1.0 - momentum)) * g
x = x + velocity
```

Now by choosing `learning_rate` and `momentum` appropriately we can simulate the CS231/NAG version (because the map (x, y) -> (xy, x(1-y)) is invertible, with inverse (a, b) -> (a+b, 1/(b/a+1))).

Similarly, if we start from the PyTorch version,

```python
velocity = mass * velocity + g
x = x - learning_rate * velocity
```

We can push the `-learning_rate` scale into the velocity sequence to get

```python
velocity = (-learning_rate * mass) * velocity - learning_rate * g
x = x + velocity
```

and now by choosing `mass` such that `-learning_rate * mass = momentum` we can simulate the CS231/NAG version.

Note this was raised a lot at https://github.com/PyCQA/pyflakes/pull/59 , but the issue of shadowing wasnt considered.

The following does not raise an error UnboundLocalError on Python 3 ; instead `e` is ` after the exception block.

```
def a():
    e = 1

    try:
        pass
    except Exception as e:
        print(e)

    print(e)

a()
```

As we can not know whether the try block will pass or which exception might fail, we can not know which local variables were discarded.

Thus the shadowing actually results in very non-deterministic behaviour, and should be considered a bug.

I know this is already implemented, but I just wanted to point out a use-case for redefining `_` that never occurred to me to point out here. Often when you have a decorator that adds a function to a registry, the name of the function is irrelevant so `_` is used. See for instance the [singledispatch](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functools.html#functools.singledispatch) decorator from the standard library (in this case, the only other natural name other than `_` is the name of the function itself, which pyflakes would warn about).  I actually saw this a lot with prompt-toolkit's keybindings ([example](https://github.com/jonathanslenders/python-prompt-toolkit/blob/master/prompt_toolkit/key_binding/bindings/emacs.py)), but is never occurred to me to point it out here. Of course, function and class definitions are special because, like loops, they require a name, but applying the rule to all names seems like a good idea if just for consistency.

First for the easy part, @naclander asked "Why would sshuttle try intercept the ssh connection packets in --method=tproxy only and not in the other methods?"

For the NAT method we use the REDIRECT target. I believe this uses connection tracking. The ssh connection is established before we setup the firewall rules. So the REDIRECT sees packets from existing connections including the existing ssh connection and ignores them because it knows it can't deal with them. It only looks at new connections.

For the TPROXY method however we use a different set of rules, I suspect these rules don't use connection tracking; so they will stuff up existing connections too.

At least that is my theory.

It would appear that 5.4 hits a PyPI file size-limit.

PyQt 5.3 weighs in around 53mb, whereas 5.4 ends up in the 90mb range. I've opened up a support ticket regarding the issue and so it *may* be resolved, but I'm not counting on it.

- https://sourceforge.net/p/pypi/support-requests/471/

An alternative would be to provide pure-PyQt5 via PyPI and have users install the Qt libraries themselves.

I haven't actually tested this but here is something you can try. The premise here is that there is a runtime that runs there and you have to trick the .NET Core SDK to run on it.

Take the runtime archive from here:
https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkID=835024

Then get the sdk archive here:
https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?linkid=841687

Copy the runtime into the sdk by doing this:

Copy the contents out of shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App\1.1.0 folder in the runtime archive into two places in the sdk archive:

shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App\1.1.0
shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App\1.0.3

That should work. If it doesn't, also do the same thing for the host folder copying the host\fxr\1.1.0\ contents into both the host\fxr\1.1.0\ and host\fxr\1.0.1\ folders in the sdk

This effectively makes it so you will be using a runtime that supports Ubuntu 16.10 to power the SDK tooling.

As I said, I don't have a 16.10 machine handy to try this but its similar to other steps I've followed to get things working in other places where the SDK isn't.

```diff

+from letsencrypt.errors import PluginError, MissingCommandlineFlag

@@ -116,12 +117,18 @@
 
     def _get_addrs(self, achall):
         """Return the Apache addresses needed for TLS-SNI-01."""
-        vhost = self.configurator.choose_vhost(achall.domain, temp=True)
         # TODO: Checkout _default_ rules.
         addrs = set()
         default_addr = obj.Addr(("*", str(
             self.configurator.config.tls_sni_01_port)))
 
+        try:
+            vhost = self.configurator.choose_vhost(achall.domain, temp=True)
+        except (PluginError, MissingCommandlineFlag):
+            # https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/pull/2906
+            addrs.add(default_addr)

         for addr in vhost.addrs:
             if "_default_" == addr.get_addr():
```

Export `CMD_DURATION` after each command executes by defining a fish function like this:

```fish
function fish_postexec --on-event fish_postexec

    set --export CMD_DURATION $CMD_DURATION
end
```

I'm not sure passing silently is a desirable behavior. If a developer has a typo in one of their `only` parameters, it is useful to communicate this to them with an error instead of hoping they notice it and requiring them debug the source of the data loss. That is how it currently works as long as the "invalid" key doesn't happen to match some data that is being dumped.

I think the bug here is that the second example isn't errorring. There is nothing in the documentation to indicate that `only` parameters that are not in the schema should act like `additional` fields and pass through without validation. I think we should proactively validate the `only` values against the fields instead of relying on missing data to indirectly cause an error.

I alluded to this by way of my previous comment:

> The reason I didn't submit a pull request with unconditional deserialization was that I was not absolutely sure that there did not exist a valid reason to deserialize a float.

However, what swayed me was the argument that two other libraries work this way explicitly - and it will be the canonical use case nearly all of the time. Another potentially compelling argument is that: absent explicit detail or documentation you'd expect a JSON float sent via HTTP to be interpreted as a string because all HTTP request bodies begin as strings (save for form data) and it would be much less expected to rely on extremely high precision floating point value coercion simply because the argument was sent unquoted as therefore as a "float".

To summarize I believe the best course of action is to defer to the package maintainer in this case.

> That's a good question, Victor.  cloudpickle uses three hooks inside

> - the "dispatch" dictionary

Sure, please take a look at the https://gist.github.com/Midnighter/7dbb09274296bc48730fb61869ff18af). Basically, classes with slots are more compact and deterministic. However, if you don't define `__slots__` in your class, Python automatically defines the `__dict__` attribute for you. If any class in your parent hierarchy does this,  it is pointless to use slots in child classes because `__dict__` is already defined. The linked StackOverflow answer in my above comment has a very extensive description and I recommend reading it when you have the time. Please note that in the notebook I also care about the `__weakref__` attribute because my code needs it but that actually doesn't really relate to this issue.

I agree this would be nice, but proof mode is currently too deeply interwoven with how expressions are built and contexts are managed. This is mostly a by-product of how contexts were used as holistically maintaining assertions. This is a while ago, but changing this infrastructure would at most give the benefit you are seeking and there may be another workable solution as follows:
 
My recommendation for something usable is to create two contexts: one for proofs/interpolants, and the other for calls that don't require proofs. You can use the "translate" methods to import expressions from one context to another, thereby keeping solver states in sync where appropriate. Of course, your infrastructure can also build terms twice, but it is not required. The Z3_translate function translates expressions from one context to another. There are also functions for translating solvers and goals.

Hey, thank you for the PR! This technique has also been termed "Layer normalization" before, see #736, #738, #762. In that case, it also contains the learnable scale and shift as in batch normalization.

> While the existing BatchNormLayer is able to implement instance normalization by setting the axes, beta and gamma arguments accordingly, it still requires a fixed batch size as learned parameters need to be initialized.

Yes, that's a bummer. Another disadvantage of using the batch normalization layer for this is that you'll need to pass extra arguments to `lasagne.layers.get_output` to disable learning and using the exponential running average statistics.
As argued in https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/736#issuecomment-241374360, I'd prefer to have a `NormLayer` as a new base class for `BatchNormLayer`, such that the `BatchNormLayer` only adds the running average statistics on top of the `NormLayer` implementation and changes the default axes. This would reduce some code duplication. I like the term "instance normalization", though, we could still use `instance_norm` for the convenience function. (I think I wouldn't use `InstanceNormLayer` for the class, because depending on the axes, it could do something else, and it would sound a bit weird to derive batch normalization from instance normalization. On the other hand, depending on the axes, `BatchNormLayer` also doesn't necessarily normalize a batch. I'm open for other arguments.)

Would you like to update your PR such that `NormLayer` includes `mean` and `gamma` and becomes a base class for `BatchNormLayer`?

In this case you have to subclass ``MinValueValidator`` and implement the logic in this subclass. It could be something like this:

```python
from django.core.validators import MinValueValidator


class MoneyValidator(MinValueValidator):
```

```
git subtree add --prefix=packages/${NAME} git@github.com:nteract/${NAME}.git master
```

This gives you the option of preserving the _full_ git history for the package repo, including contributor credit, blame, and whatnot. If you just want a single commit it accept a `--squash` argument too.

well, I guess the issue is that "by default" import of a module is not sufficient (on purpose) to have it cited -- the idea is that we should cite modules which were actually used, i.e. some also duecredit decorated within them functionality was called.  Otherwise pretty much import of any python module which imports a few dozens of other modules just to make their API available, would lead to a citation of numerous modules even if they aren't actually used.  
For some modules though, where we know that if they are imported they are likely to be used (e.g. numpy), we also add `cite_module=True` argument, thus causing a citation to appear even if no other duecredit-decorated method was called within it.  You could try and see if your module gets cited ;-)  or you could `due.cite` some function within your module and invoke it to get both the citation for the function and then for the module on top of it.

@drmarkwslater 

### bash environment settings
You can't source this .bashrc and have Ganga setup from what I've seen there is far too much conflict in the environment even for this basically small script (I suspect the ```_=env``` is to blame but I'm not too interested in debugging this.)
The simplest solution is to correctly set the env before the ```dirac-proxy-*``` calls as this is the only time it's needed and a source operation such as this has _minimal_ overhead.

As for reproducing the problem with the proxy, yes if everything is setup correctly then it will work, but this is clearly __not__ correct on __all__ systems. The fact it works somewhere and not others isn't really a good reason to keep using GLITE as per the default configs on cvmfs imo and neither is it correct to call the ```dirac-*``` tools without a correct environment as I was doing initially.
We need to have something setup which doesn't use the GLITE system imo and that uses the env file from the grid. I was being lazy and just sharing my '.ini' which I'm using now but for completeness:

```
[LCG]
EDG_ENABLE = False
GLITE_ENABLE = False
[Configuration]
RUNTIME_PATH = GangaDirac
[defaults_GridCommand]
info = source /cvmfs/ganga.cern.ch/dirac_ui/envfile && /cvmfs/ganga.cern.ch/dirac_ui/scripts/dirac-proxy-info
init = source /cvmfs/ganga.cern.ch/dirac_ui/envfile && /cvmfs/ganga.cern.ch/dirac_ui/scripts/dirac-proxy-init -g lsst_user -M
[DIRAC]
DiracEnvFile = /cvmfs/ganga.cern.ch/dirac_ui/envfile
```

This has the strong advantage of just working out of the box and doesn't confuse different parts of the codebase.

### Dirac job outputs

As for job outputs. No, the ```LocalFile``` outputtfiles are tarred up from the working directory as outputsandbox as per the job description. If this wasn't working I'd be seeing no ```std.out``` but I get the sandbox as expected.
```DiracFile```s are tried to be created from the job at the end of running by having DIRAC upload them to some temporary storage solution (typically something like ```GridPPSandboxSE```). This is managed entirely by the pilot and not by us which allows for things like the failure to upload to trigger a new upload to a different SE without risk of losing the file.

We a-priori have no control over where DIRAC decides to upload this file at the end of a job as we just request that DIRAC does it and that we hand off without running any code to do this.
(There is a bug that we ship the code to manually upload a DIRAC file ourselves using a WN script which shouldn't be shipped to DIRAC as it's never called. This could be abused to force sending of the files to somewhere more sensible.)

### DiracFile put

I'm still left with #416 as I see no easy way from the DIRAC API to determine the SE for a known DIRAC config. I can code something up but it runs the risk of being deprecated in some future update.
Yes a short-term solution is to hard-code some SE names (which probably won't change) but this becomes yet another thing we have to maintain. It would be better is this was automatic.
But remember that these are not forks in the Linux sense. It is all just a single python process.

@alexanderrichards  What should happen and what largely does happen across most of the codebase:

```_data``` is used to store the index cache information i.e. the stuff used by the ```_display``` method behind the call to ```jobs```
This is typically None or `{}` for a job which is fully-loaded in memory but there have been occasions when it's not which just adds confusion imo.

The actual data of the job object when it's loaded from memory should be loaded into the same place as-if the job has been created that session. So a loaded job should correctly have populated the job objects ```__dict__```.

When a job is loaded from disk ```_data``` should be cleared and then the object should be fully populated.
(There confusion in the fact that it's not always empty and I strongly think that if this is largely meta-data-ish then it should be constructed on the fly rather than storing copied information from the job.
The ```_data``` object always takes precedent over a fully-loaded job as per design dating back before 6.1.13 by a long way so if this ever becomes out of sync with the true object funny behaviour can happen)

When a job is not loaded the getter methods will attempt to use information from ```_data``` to satisfy the request which seems perfectly fine until you get things like ```hasattr(myJob, 'display:id')``` sometimes returning true which is odd behaviour imo.

I think this makes sense.

@hustshawn the basic idea is to not use the scrapy-splash stuffs, but to make use of the functionalities of the scrapy-cluster + scrapy.

The followings are mainly for PoC without optimization. 

1. we need to reuse the feeding capability of scrapy-cluster, so I add extra "attrs" in the json request

> python kafka_monitor.py feed '{"url": "https://www.test.com", "appid":"testapp", "crawlid":"09876abc", "useragent": "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 6.1; WOW64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/56.0.2924.87 Safari/537.36", **"attrs": {"splash": "1"**}, "spiderid": "test"}'

the "splash": 1 is to tell that the reuqest needs to go to Splash with Http API directly

2. add a downloader middleware into the scrapy-cluster and in the "process_request", if we detect the splash attribute, insert some necessary meta

```
splash_meta = request.meta[self.splash_meta_name]

        args = splash_meta.setdefault('args', {})
        splash_url = urljoin(self.splash_base_url, self.default_endpoint)
        args.setdefault('splash_url', splash_url)

        # only support POST api to Splash now
        args.setdefault('http_method', 'POST')

        body = json.dumps({"url": request.meta['url'], "wait": 5, "timeout": 10}, sort_keys=True)
        args.setdefault('body', body)

        headers = Headers({'Content-Type': 'application/json'})
        args.setdefault('headers', headers)
```

3. When the request arrives at scrapy downloader, in the HTTP11DownloadHandler's download_reuqest:
we need to replace the request:

```
def download_request(self, request, spider):
        """Return a deferred for the HTTP download"""
        agent = ScrapyAgent(contextFactory=self._contextFactory, pool=self._pool,
            maxsize=getattr(spider, 'download_maxsize', self._default_maxsize),
            warnsize=getattr(spider, 'download_warnsize', self._default_warnsize))

        if "splash" in request.meta:
            # we got a Splash forward request now
            splash_args = request.meta['splash']['args']
            new_splash_request = request.replace(
                url = splash_args['splash_url'],
                method = splash_args['http_method'],
                body = splash_args['body'],
                headers = splash_args['headers'],
                priority = request.priority
            )
            return agent.download_request(new_splash_request)
        else:
            return agent.download_request(request)
```

Slightly tangentially, I was thinking there is a use case for lossless WARC extraction spec, where there is a one-to-one representation between WARCs and data on disk, such that headers and metadata is preserved, eg. A record for 'http://example.com/index.html` could be extracted as:

```
+ example.com/
    index.html
    .headers/
         http
         warc
```
with headers store in a hidden `.headers` dir, so that a user could open index.html, but a tool can still access the headers and not lose fidelity. This may be a useful extension to "unwarcit" @despens 

On the other hand, @shawnmjones , if you *do* want to create WARC records, you can use the `WARC-Source-URI` and `WARC-Creation-Date` header extensions to indicate that the record is an extracted from a different archive.
The source URI is where it was extracted from and creation date is the date you actually created the record. Webrecorder and pywb make use of these headers when extracting from other archives.

Hi all, 

I've been working on this issue.  My proposed revamp is this:

* Remove "connection strings" entirely from the codebase

* Replace each instance of a "connection string" in the codebase with a new type of object, called `Endpoint`

* Change API's for `%%spark` magic to use three separate arguments to capture an endpoint: one for URL, one for username, one for password

Since it could be more cumbersome to use 3 arguments instead of one argument argument, I believe that the best way to make this tractable would be to add "named endpoints" to the library as a feature, in the same way that named endpoints are now a feature.  So, after this work is done, you might create a Livy session like this:

    # Create an endpoint called `endp`
    %spark add -e endp --url https://example.com/livy --user admin --password 
    # Create a session called `session` at the endpoint `endp`
    %spark add -s session -l python -e endp

This proposed API uses named arguments rather than positional arguments, which the current `%%spark` API favors.  (This proposal uses the same command `add` for adding endpoints and sessions, but there could be different commands like `add-session` and `add-endpoint`, for example.)

This could also integrate with the session/endpoint management widgets so that you could (for example) use the Endpoint creation widget to create an endpoint called `e` and then run `%spark add -s s -l python -e e` to easily add a session at that endpoint.

As far as I know, there are no tasks which deterministically take a long time to complete. As a result, the second-best thing we can do is to launch a task which saturates some systm resource, such as CPU time or network bandwidth, and try to make a test that completes quickly enough.

I strongly caution against writing a test that saturates available network bandwidth, especially if targeting a resource that's freely made available to the public (like `repos.fedorapeople.org`) for public benefit.

A task which saturates some other resource could be much better. As an example, when Pulp 2 parses the metadata in a large OpenSUSE repository, it takes tens of minutes to complete, even on modern hardware. If the Pulp 3 plugin improves enough, perhaps a similar approach could be taken for this test.

Here's a brief proposal. We can continue submitting pull requests as we currently do, but the standard for pull requests would be as so:

* A pull request should pass all automated linters. Travis already checks this automatically, but this can also be done locally with the various make targets.
* The tests added by a pull request should pass. The various functions provided by [`pulp_smash.selectors`](http://pulp-smash.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/pulp_smash.selectors.html) are of special use in this regard.
* A pull request should target a single issue. For example, if a given pull request both adds tests and fixes a bug in a utility function, those are two separate concerns, and separate pull requests can be submitted for each. As a side effect, this means that each pull request should contain one commit.

If a pull request satisfies those requirements, and if there are no other outstanding issues, [1] then the reviewer can add a second commit addressing whatever concerns or nitpicks they have, rebase the pair of commits on master, verify the sanity of the result (Does the code still satisfy the linters? Do the affected tests still pass? Do the pair of commits target a single issue?), and merge.

* Commit authorship is preserved. Both of the individuals involved in adding a feature will have their work listed.
* Commit integrity is preserved. Commits can reflect the actual output of individuals, instead of being tweaked invisibly.
* The "linters must pass" requirement is currently automated, and the "tests must pass" requirement can be automated. That's two of three.

There is one downside to this approach that sticks out to me, and it's that the commit history will be less straightforward. This makes using `revert`, `bisect`, `log`, and all manner of other tools harder to do. However, two commits are still pretty reasonable. (We can try experimenting with `git merge --no-ff` when adding submitter/reviewer pairs of commits. That might make the pairs of commits more clear, but the full implications of that approach aren't clear to me yet.)

[1] What qualifies as an "outstanding issue?" Examples might include:

* A commit adds a 10MB binary blob to the repository without exceedingly good reason. Remember that git keeps all data forever, whether or not it is used.
* A commit adds a test that makes dozens of concurrent requests to a publicly available service such as docker hub.

This "outstanding issue" clause is intentionally vague. There's just now way that all issues can be foreseen, and leaving subjective wiggle room seems wise.

The `SerializerMutation` tries to define an output type from the model of a serializer. If no `DjangoObjectType` exists for that model it will silently return `None` as the type. This will later lead to an error.

Actually I would like to see a `SerializerMutation` that does not require model types to be defined. I don't overall like very much, that the whole library is very tightly coupled with the django ORM. There is also a need for not exposing every model field to the outside world.

Yep, the theoretical complexity is O(n^2), but, in practice, it is linear. The thing is, you shouldn't use spaCy pipeline for that but rather `WMD.nearest_neighbors` method. It has many clever tricks to reduce the number of comparisons for every document. For example, in our production we've got 120k "documents", each about 200 tf-idf weighted "words", and NNs for every "document" evaluate only 100-200 others (quotes here because our domain is not NLP). Besides, to do massive all2all comparisons, you should use `WMD.cache_centroids`.

That being said, this is still not perfect at calculating the complete similarity matrix, but at least Spark-eable.

Hi Anil,

You can use a local model by providing the name of a local directory. Please see https://github.com/tensorflow/hub/blob/master/tensorflow_hub/resolver.py#L543

The SavedModel produced by retrain.py cannot be used as a module. Instead, the SavedModel is the whole model that can be used for serving.

The retrain.py script does not currently support the continuation of training from an existing checkpoint, which is what you're looking for. To achieve that, you can replace the line https://github.com/tensorflow/hub/blob/master/examples/image_retraining/retrain.py#L1031 and the one thereafter by:

tf.train.Saver().restore(sess, CHECKPOINT_NAME)

Then a subsequent run of the script will initialize the variables from the training checkpoint of the previous run (when CHECKPOINT_NAME still exists on your disk).

Hope this helps! S

@zhanglongxia here is what I would suggest for tracking of the offset/length for `MeshHeader` received message.
- Let's keep the `Message` APIs exactly as they are. I don't think you need a change.
- For `MeshHeader` frames, we can use a different queue to store a `Message` containing only the first fragment (to keep the fragment tag, and priority associated with the message) - or alternatively you can use a queue of  `PriorityEntry` struct (like what you had earlier) instead of a queue of `Message`s.
- For any received `MeshHeader` fragmented message, we can get both length and offset from the fragment lowpan header. So there is no need to track it in the message. I guess we need the length and offset to determine if the frame is the last fragment so we can delete the corresponding entry from the queue.

Application-layer provisioning through the MeshCoP protocol defined in Section 8.9.3 of the Thread Specification is not implemented at this time.  This is an enhancement we can look into if requested.  As always, we welcome contributions as well.

The provisioning URL information provides a means to inform the Commissioner of an application-specific Commissioner application desired by the Joiner.  The JOIN_FIN.rsp message allows the Commissioner to inform the Joiner whether it has the correct Commissioner application.

As you noted, using the same DTLS session to provision both the Thread and application layers is optional.  Reusing the DTLS session provides the following abilities:

1. Reduce overhead associated with establishing a separate DTLS session for the application layer.
2. Allow the joining device to choose not to participate in the Thread network if the application layer cannot be provisioned.

That said, there are many good reasons to have the device join the network then attempt to provision the application layer separately.  Ultimately, the choice is application/solution specific.

In the FakeJunOSDevice class we need to add one more method for the configuration getters since in PyE they are treated as a different type of requests. The method receives two params: get_cmd (an etree type object with a structure like <configuration><protocols><bgp><group/></bgp></protocols></configuration>) and options (a dictionary with options).
Since there's one single RPC for all possible configuration-related requests I thought it would be less messy to have one single text file with the config (where to append parts of the config -- and possibly at some point it might look like a whole device configuration). In the same time I am thinking that we don't deal with one single type of device but with a variety with different features (switches that do not know BGP etc.) and it would be more handy to have files named based on the format of the get_cmd tree (e.g.: configuration__protocols__bgp__group___bgp___protocols___configuration.txt). This data set is from the USGS with the measurement data for all the specimens collected over decades (long projects at the USGS sometimes). So in this case they converted over to the Cal Tech system around 2005. We have the data files from those, but before that they are either on paper or some dos floppies. We may try and get those eventually, but I wanted to get the partial set of data available. This will happen a lot I think for people not using PmagPy for analysis (everyone but Berkeley and SIO for now I think). Showing how PmagPy and MagIC can be used on people's own data is a better way to convince them to use PmagPy for their analysis and easy upload into MagIC. 

Also, there are studies where people take set of samples from a lava flow and a site mean was determined. Later during the same study, another lava is sampled far away, a set of samples are taken, and another site mean determined. Eventually using the paleomagnetic directions and geochemistry of some of the sites are determined to have erupted from the same volcanic eruption and are combined in an average. This is also called a site. Thinking about it now, the result_type should be set to 'a' (for average) for this type of averaged site instead of usual 'i' (individual) for the other sites. I will modify my sites table to have an 'a' for the averages sites.

In the sites.txt file I gave you the 'i' sites have alphanumeric names like BL8097 while the averages sites (which I need to change to 'a') have names the are used for the whole eruption that can cover thousands of square kilometers (eg. Basalt of Barnes Road).

There should not be any samples and/or specimens that do not have a parent site and location in the sites.txt and location.txt files for this data set. I did not check for this closely, so I may have missed where my scripts were not set up right. Is it a problem for pmag_gui to have sites that have no sample/specimens? For the case where a researcher has not grouped some measurements and specimens into sites and locations, putting them all in a default called 'unknown' and printing a warning seems like a good solution.

If ignoring sites that have no specimen data is a pain to fix, I am not sure what to do. Many MagIC data sets will be like this and it would be nice if people can use demag_gui to look at the data downloaded from MagIC. Not sure the best way to deal with this.

Actually, it turns out SendGrid does the same thing if you let them generate their own Message-ID: they use the same Message-ID for all individual recipient emails in the batch. So there's no way to avoid this behavior with SendGrid's current functionality.

And the "duplicate" Message-IDs are probably OK, because the sender get to decide whether or not two emails are "the same message" under [RFC 2822](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2822#section-3.6.4) [emphasis added]:

>    Note: There are many instances when messages are "changed", but those
>    changes do not constitute a new instantiation of that message, and
>    therefore the message would not get a new message identifier.  ...
>    In all cases, **it is the meaning that the sender
>    of the message wishes to convey (i.e., whether this is the same
>    message or a different message) that determines whether or not the
>    "Message-ID:" field changes,** not any particular syntactic difference
>    that appears (or does not appear) in the message.

> Is the global `CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS` check completely necessary? It ends up mixing global variables and target-specific state. Modern CMake is trying very hard to move away from global variables.

Yes.  We aren't setting them, passing them, or encouraging cmake code to use them: we're only respecting if they are set, which is typically going to happen from outside cmake (e.g. invoking `CXXFLAGS="-whatever" cmake ..`).  The perfect case for this is @ghisvail's: he wants to disable -flto globally for a build, because it's broken with his current $CXX.  We're providing no way at all to override the option to a package builder aside from "patch the cmake source" which really isn't reasonable.

> The ability to turn off LTO is already available with the NO_EXTRAS option.

To be blunt, `NO_EXTRAS` seems like a bad solution: it's one option that controls 5 different, largely unrelated things:
- LTO
- `-fvisibility=hidden`
- stripping of binaries
- big object compilation on Windows
- multithreaded compilation on Windows

Moreover it's only available to cmake code, but the main point of respecting CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS is to respect the person invoking cmake, *not* the cmake code calling `pybind11_add_module`.

Those options really should be split up into independent `pybind_add_module` options (e.g. `NO_LTO`, `NO_HIDDEN`, etc.), but that's outside the scope of this PR.  And even if they were split up, it still wouldn't address the issue of the cmake invoker being allowed to override compilation flags with CXXFLAGS.

> Is there any possibility to merge `_pybind11_check_cxx_and_linker_flags` and `_pybind11_return_if_cxx_and_linker_flags_work` into a single macro just to make it easier to follow? Reading the LTO selection is a bit tricky with 3 levels to go through.

It's possible, but it would result in more code: the main point of abstracting the bits that are in the function is that cmake deals with setting up a variable stack so that we don't need to save the local values of `CMAKE_REQUIRED_{QUIET,LIBRARIES}` and restore them afterwards.  Of course this could be done manually inside the macro, but it would increase the macro by more than the size of the function, which I think would end up harder to follow than it is now.  The macro also can't be a function because it really is working as a macro--encapsulating the return and "return value" (i.e. parent scope sets) from the caller's function.

Hi Wenzel,

yes I guess it is. We have been using it internally for a few things and it seems to hold up. I'll send an official pull request later today.

You might also be interested in https://github.com/lsst-dm/pybind11-1/pull/3/files. This is a proposed extension to allow exceptions to derive from Python's Exception type. Which allows them to be raised and caught with the standard `except Exception as e` syntax from Python.
This extension is still a bit rough around the edges and we have opted not to use it for LSST yet, instead relying on a workaround solution that calls back to Python with the C++ wrapped exception type to generate an equivalent Python exception which forwards to the embedded C++ type. This workaround, although less elegant, was chosen because the exceptions are nested in different trees on the C++ and Python side which would also necessitate multiple inheritance in pybind11 which I believe isn't supported.

But it might still be very relevant for other users. The main drawback in the current extension design is that it increases the size of every object by three pointers. These are strictly speaking only required for casting of exceptions but there is no good way to distinguish between object types and only insert them when needed. I tried using `enable_if` in `instance_essentials` but that creates troubles with weakrefs.
I might think about a better way to fix this if I have time, but don't expect anything there soon since it is not high priority for us.

Cheers,

Hi @szepeviktor, the account key does *not* allow you to recover your private key, because the certificate authority was, by design, never in possession of your private key. The only copy of the private key would have been on your own server. Therefore, in this situation you will need to issue a new certificate instead.

It should be possible, however, to use your account key to revoke your previously-issued certificate using the `certbot revoke` command.

> the letsencrypt logs show at 3am and then again at 12 noon and also midnight - why is that?

The Certbot packages on Debian based systems comes with a cron job/systemd timer to run Certbot at noon and midnight (with a 1 hour random offset from that time). The crontab is placed at `/etc/cron.d/certbot`. Can you provide a link to the documentation you were following that failed to mention this so we can try and get it updated?

> Why would the server restart at 06.25 (only) everyday 

I'm not sure about this one. As you pointed out, the long delay between Certbot running and Apache starting is very strange. Additionally, Certbot reloads your server only when it needs to which is when it renews your certificate. Most times when `certbot renew` is run, it should simply check the expiration date of your certs, see there's nothing to do, and exit.

A few questions for you:

1. Looking at your logs, can you tell if most runs of Certbot are issuing a new cert or just exiting as I described above? If the former, have you included `force-renewal` in a [Certbot configuration file](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#configuration-file)?
2. What is the closest timestamp in the Certbot logs to Apache restarting? This will probably be in your logs from 3.10 at the bottom of the file. In every case where we would restart Apache, more logging output should come after that, so I want to rule out the very unlikely possibility of Certbot taking 3+ hours to run.
3. Do you have old enough Apache logs to verify that Apache restarting at 6:25 came with the installation of Certbot and wasn't happening before?

The problem isn't that cerbot is setting the directive to something other than what I have used, it's that certbot is duplicating an existing directive. Even with `server_names_hash_bucket_size 128;` in my `nginx.conf`, certbot will crash nginx with the same error message.

As a simple test, if I forget certbot for a moment and add the following two lines to `nginx.conf`:

```
server_names_hash_bucket_size 128;
server_names_hash_bucket_size 128;
```

Then I check the configuration syntax:

```
# nginx -t
nginx: [emerg] "server_names_hash_bucket_size" directive is duplicate in /etc/nginx/conf.d/01-server-names.conf:2
```

So nginx will fail a configuration check if the same directive is defined twice *regardless of the value of that directive*. This means that if you need to set `server_names_hash_bucket_size` to any value, even the same as used by certbot, you cannot use the nginx plugin.

Right now at https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/acme/acme/challenges.py#L237 we are raising a simple `errors.Error` whenever someone try to use dns challenge without having `dnspython`. That's difficult to handle cause `Error` is super generic. We could then:

a) make use of Python 3 `raise from` using the `raise_from` syntax from `six` and chain the Error exception the ImportError caused by `dnspython` and check if there is ImportError in the traceback of the exception.

c) Just catch `errors.Error` when using `simple_very` and interpret those as if the dependency was missing no matter what

Hi @drandreaskrueger ,

Do you recall if the raiden node was restarted? That explains the error message.

The first time the node started it tried to register its `ip:port` pair with the smart contact, the node was restarted but the transaction was not mined in time, at the second time the same transaction was sent to the ethereum node resulting in that error.

We can change the rpc client to check if a transaction for registration is in-flight and wait for it to complete instead of trying a new one on top.
Since we are not checking the nonce anymore the function `updateTransfer` should only be called by the counter party.

Remote execution is now fixed. Example:

```
$ cat test.sos
[10]
input: for_each=[{'a': range(10)}, {'b': range(3)}]

task: concurrent=True
run:
    echo "a = ${a}, b = ${b}"
    sleep ${a + b}
```

run with

```
sos run test -q linux
```

`stop_if` is designed to skip an input group and is not meant to stop the execution of the workflow, which is performed by  `fail_if`.

Yes, there will be a race condition there since we use asynchronous messages to update global control state. Any updates that are still in flight to the control state when the local scheduler dies will be lost. We only guarantee deterministic execution for tasks whose execution edge updates have gone through; the other tasks will re-execute but not necessarily in the same order.

Please don't open a single thread for multiple issues. This is impossible to track for the developers in an issue tracker with that many open issues.

Regarding 1:
The cam4 plugin currently only matches .com URLs, as you can see here.
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/0.8.1/src/streamlink/plugins/cam4.py#L10

Regarding 2:
Duplicate of #1271...

I don't know whether I want to close this thread or not, since you're requesting multiple plugins at once. One of them already had a couple of other discussions, but the plugin was never finalized by its creator as it looks like.

The amount of NSFW plugin requests/issues/discussions here is increasing rapidly and it's getting too much for my taste. Especially from newly created github accounts. This is slowly becoming a porn site ripping tool, which we should not let happen. Maybe we should move NSFW plugins into a different repo or so.

Hi @nanuxbe thanks for your detailed application. 

I think I am looking a functionality drf-schema-adapter offers. 

Let me describe my requirement shortly:

Currently I already have the prototype of a dynamic form, created by drf static serializer/viewset with a multiple of select fields, where field options dynamically is adapted by using filtersets based on the selection of other select fields.

The filtering is done against a intermediate table that contains the values valid for the select field options.

As we currently have to maintain the serializers / viewsets / filtersets by hand and there are more than 30 serializers involved my idea is to perform the creation of serializers, viewsets, filtersets dynamicly.
I am thinking of creating an api entry (`/api/form1/{data, options}`) where options is the entry for nested routes to intermediate model (`form1/options/{selectfield1, selectfield2, ...}`). `form1/data` is the endpoint for form `CRUD`. 

The foreign key fields where a serializer is neccessary should be autodetected by iterating the form base serializer model and looking for `ForeignKey` fields. The filtering should be based on the `to_field` of the intermediate model. The Filterset should be created dynamically, based on the select fields involved and filtering against the intermediate model without the current field.

Do you think this is a usecase that could be realised on `drf_auto_endpoint`?

1. The example you link in pt 2 also has square points. What you probably want is to give it a texture for the `map` property of the material. E.g. something disc shaped.

2. The `Lut` code is part of the example, and is available here: https://github.com/mrdoob/three.js/blob/master/examples/js/math/Lut.js . It is just a utility for builing a vertex attribute called 'color', as used here:

```js
geometry.addAttribute( 'color', new THREE.BufferAttribute( new Float32Array( lutColors ), 3 ) );
```

If you are using a `BufferGeometry` I would use the examples for that on how to add an attribute, and add an attribute `color` from e.g. a numpy array that you fill with the wanted colors.

Seems that pytorch extended the set of `DataLoader` arguments with `worker_init_fn`: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/utils/data/dataloader.py#L375-L377. This allows the user to write a custom function that will get executed on each worker: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/utils/data/dataloader.py#L49

That is a desired behaviour (to my opinion), for 2 reasons - 1) when things work, user should not need to go ssh-ing into instances :) and 2) when the key is specified, that generates an extra security group, and there is no mechanism to clean those up; and eventually AWS caps those. So I wanted it to be somewhat of a conscious effort to add a ssh keypair to the worker. 
But what we can also do is make it configurable from config file.

> Wouldn't the producer of a serialized model play the role of the "caller" and the consumer (the runtime that executes) a model play the role of the "callee" ?

Yeah, I agree with this.

> A single model can reference and make use of multiple OperatorSets. Are there any constraints here? Can a model use both "com.acme.CNN:3" and "com.acme.RNN:5" mixing different versions of the same OperatorSet (domain)? Or, are such questions outside the scope of this?

You are NOT allowed to mix together multiple versions of an operator set. While technically nothing is stopping us from adding this to spec, it's bad behavior and we shan't encourage it.

One extension Don and I debated was whether or not to allow specifying a version on individual NodeProto. This would short circuit the normal resolution process and let you refer to a legacy operator. On balance, we decided not to add it, although it is a coherent extension.

> If an implementation claims to support some specific OperatorSets, does it mean that it should be able to handle models that mix operators from these different OperatorSets, or is that outside the scope of this standard?

Yes, mixes of different operator sets (e.g., *domains*) should be handled.

> Why did you make individual reference transformation tests instead of using something similar to PREPROCESSORS_INDEPENDENT_SAMPLES += list(add_different_reference(EMSC, "reference", SMALL_COLLAGEN[0:1])) line and then rely on general preprocessor test to catch incorrect matching (of course you would have to fake a collagen reference somehow)? I think the preprocessors were passing the general test but not working for the desired interpolation functionality, so I was trying to test interpolating incompatible references and domain transform in one go. I'm not fixed on this approach though, the tests could definitely use some simplification.

So to be clear about what I was really trying to test: the dataset I was playing with had a reference with a slightly larger energy window than the sample, and I want to test the case where you put the sample set (with auto-interpolated capability) on the input and the non-matching reference on the reference.

> Could you explain what did you want to do with different kind of references (many:many, many:1, many:subset reference)? Do I understand correctly, that a possible use cases would be that you could either have a reference of a single row

So:
- for the **many:1** case, you have a set of sample single beams (say from a pt-by-pt map) and a single reference single beam and you want to calculate absorbance or whatever
- for the **many:many** case you have a set of sample single beams (say from a set of measurements) and a equal-sized and ordered set of reference single beams and you want to calculate the absorbance for each as appropriate. This would also be the case for a single-tile FPA sample/reference single beam.
- for the **many:subset** case, the case I'm thinking of is a mosaic FPA sample single beams and you want to reference to a single-tile reference FPA set. This is a bit more tricky so I decided not to handle this yet (see Non-goals).

Your tip about using `repr()` to get the `compute_value` of the domain is really helpful!
